reviews.text:
Review of “Weighted Ensemble Self-Supervised Learning” (ICLR 2023)

Paper Summary  
This paper investigates how ensembling can be effectively incorporated into modern self-supervised learning (SSL) pipelines.  The authors focus on ensembling the projection heads and codebooks—i.e., the “throw-away” components of SSL methods such as DINO and MSN—while keeping a single shared encoder.  They introduce a family of weighted cross-entropy losses that place data-dependent importance weights on each head–codebook pair and explore several weighting schemes (UNIF, PROB, ENT).  Experiments on ImageNet-1K show that their approach (in particular, ENT weighting with 4–16 heads) consistently boosts 1-shot, 2-shot, 5-shot, 1%‐data, k-NN, and full-data linear evaluation for both DINO∗ and MSN∗ architectures with ViT-S/16, ViT-B/16, and ViT-B/8 backbones.  Transfer to nine downstream datasets further confirms these gains.  Importantly, the ensemble heads are used only during training and incur no extra cost at inference.

Strengths  
1. Straightforward Implementation with Compelling Gains  The proposed method simply replicates projection heads and codebooks, incorporates a minor extension to the SSL loss, and leaves the encoder unchanged.  This design makes it easy to add to existing DINO, MSN, or similar codebases without architectural changes or extra inference cost.  Empirically, ENT‐weighted ensembles yield up to +7.5 p.p. in 1-shot and +6.3 p.p. in 1%‐data evaluation (Table 1, 1%/1-shot), and up to +5.5 p.p. in 1-shot for ViT-S/16 (Table 2).  

2. Thorough Experimental Study  The paper conducts an extensive ablation on (hψ, µ)-ensembles:  
   • Where to ensemble (projection head only vs. codebook only vs. both).  
   • How to weight (UNIF, PROB, ENT, ENT-ST, and several other variants in Appendix D).  
   • Number of heads (2–32), entropy weighting temperature γ, and projection head size.  
   • Analysis of ensemble diversity via code‐assignment similarity (Fig. 4, Figs. 8–11).  
   These analyses clearly demonstrate that data-dependent entropy weighting (ENT) produces the most diverse heads and correlates with downstream gains.

3. Reproducibility and Strong Baselines  The authors supply detailed pseudocode (Appx A), full hyperparameters for DINO∗ and MSN∗ (Appx B.2.2), and rigorous reproduction of prior art.  They retune projection heads (3×1024 MLP) and normalization techniques (Sinkhorn vs. centering) to strengthen baselines, achieving DINO∗ that outperforms the original DINO on few-shot metrics by 2–6 p.p. (Table 5) and MSN∗ that surpasses public MSN (Table 6).  The thorough reporting of both retuned baselines and published numbers underscores the robustness of their findings.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty in the Core Idea  Ensembling classifiers is a well-known paradigm in supervised learning, and extending it to projection heads/codebooks in SSL is a natural step.  While the analysis of weighting schemes and diversity is valuable, the paper’s core insight—replicate heads, weight their losses, and combine—may be viewed as an incremental application of ensembling rather than a fundamentally new learning algorithm.  

2. Upstream Evaluation on ImageNet Overlooked  Although the paper excels at few-shot, k-NN, full-data linear, and transfer evaluations, there is little discussion of how ensembling might affect pretext training metrics (e.g., SSL loss curves, convergence speed) or standard unsupervised clustering metrics on ImageNet (without labels).  A few additional plots of training loss, codebook usage (e.g., how quickly SK converges across heads), or augment invariance measures could help illustrate whether ensembling yields more robust representations beyond downstream classification performance.

Additional Comments & Suggestions  
– Discussion of Encoder-Level Ensembling  The authors mention that ensembling the encoder rω is left for future work.  A brief discussion of the anticipated trade-offs (compute/memory vs. representation diversity) and possible techniques (e.g., partial sharing or branching at intermediate layers) would enrich the paper’s perspective.  

– Applicability to Other SSL Paradigms  The method is applied only to clustering-based SSL (DINO, MSN).  It would be helpful to see, even in passing, how the approach might extend to contrastive (SimCLR), masked modeling (MAE), or prediction-based SSL (BYOL), perhaps by ensembling prediction heads or decoder layers.  

– Hyperparameter Sensitivity  While Appendix D catalogs many weighting functions, it remains unclear how sensitive ENT is to the choice of γ across architectures.  Figure 3b addresses this for ViT-S/16, but a small table summarizing optimal γ for each backbone (S/16 vs. B/16 vs. B/8) would assist practitioners in tuning.  

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, Reproducibility  
Overall, the paper is well-written, logically organized, and free of major technical gaps.  The use of maximum-likelihood notation (Sec. 2) provides a unifying lens, and the pseudocode (Appx A) plus hyperparameter tables (Appx B) ensure that other researchers can readily build on this work.  While the core idea is an expected extension of ensembling, the systematic study of data-dependent weighting schemes, ensemble diversity, and their direct benefits to SSL represents a solid empirical contribution.

Final Recommendation  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper delivers a practical, reproducible method that convincingly improves state-of-the-art SSL models on a range of downstream tasks.  Although the novelty of “ensembling throw-away parts” is modest, the thorough experiments, clear exposition, and consistent gains—especially in few-shot regimes—justify acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper systematically studies the ensemble of self-supervised learning. Ensemble has been one of the oldest time-proven strategies in machine learning and has been well-studied in supervised learning. Here, the authors introduce ensemble into the field of SSL. They mainly consider ensembling the projection head and codebook and leaving the encode part as future work. This proposed method is straightforward with rich analysis and proper results.

Strength And Weaknesses:
## Strength
(1) The proposed method is straightforward and easy to implement with compelling results.

(2) I believe this paper includes some important and thorough experimental work on ensemble of self-supervised models.

(3) Detailed hyperparameters and training configurations are provided, improving the reproducibility.

## Weakness
(1) The ensembling of the projection head and codebook is a natural extension of the classifier ensemble in supervised models. It is not too surprising to see a performance improvement. 

(2) This paper mainly evaluated ensemble on transfer learning, while the performance on upstream tasks (Imagenet) has been overlooked.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Solid contribution.

Summary Of The Review:
I believe this paper, albeit short on technical novelty, does provide a sufficiently novel and important empirical evaluation and details analysis of self-supervised learning ensembling methods and could be accepted - assuming the authors can address the issues I have detailed in my review.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the benefits of ensembling for self-supervised learning methods. The paper proposes to ensemble the “non-representation” part of the SSL models, and proposes different weighting schemes. Extensive experiments are conducted to analyze and evaluate the proposed ensemble method and weighting schemes, which lead to improvements over the non-ensemble models.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- This could be a pretty timely work that explores ensembling for self-supervised learning models. I like the idea of only ensembling the non-representation part as that incurs little training overhead, plus no extra cost for downstream tasks.
- I appreciate that the authors included the analysis on the ensemble diversity. This gives a deeper understanding of the effectiveness of different weighting schemes, and could potentially guide the designs of future weighting schemes.
- The paper includes thorough analysis and ablations on the proposed methods, and shows strong empirical results compared to the baselines and SOTA.

Weaknesses:
- The connection between the Maximum Likelihood view of SSL and the proposed ensemble method design is not very clear to me. The presentation could be improved with stronger connections between Sec.2 and Sec.3.
- While the paper does a good job in evaluating a variety of ablations on the proposed ensemble method, it seems to lack other possible baselines for ensembling, e.g., instead of only ensembling the projection head, one baseline is to consider ensembling the base models. It’d be good to compare this baseline to understand whether there is a trade-off between the benefits of ensembling and the cost for extra parameters. In general, the paper should include comparisons to the other related ensemble baseline mentioned in Sec. 4.

Other questions/comments:
- Can one use the proposed ensembling method on pre-trained SSL models (e.g. DINO)? What’s the performance difference of that to applying ensembling in the pre-training from scratch? I think this is a relevant question to look into since there are already a lot of publicly available pre-trained SSL models, and it’d be best if the ensembling technique can be applied on top of these pre-trained models and still offer improvements.
- How does one set $\gamma$ for ENT in practice? From Figure 3.b, it seems to affect the performance pretty much.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
- Clarity: The paper is overall clearly written.
- Quality and Reproducibility: The paper includes quite comprehensive experimental results, especially with careful treatments on the baseline methods. Experimental details are provided in the paper and the authors promise to release code later on. The results looks convincing and should be reproducible.
- Novelty: Ensembling for SSL models, especially in the pre-training stage, does not seem to have been studied in the literature. The proposed ensembling method and weighting scheme appears to be novel and more importantly, lead to decent performance improvements.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper studies a timely topic on ensembling for SSL models. The proposed method is simple and could fit with a variety of SSL models. While the paper can be made better by comparing to more related ensembling baselines to guide practical use, the current presented empirical results is quite comprehensive on its own, and the usefulness of the proposed method is well-justified by the empirical results.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper studies a timely topic on ensembling for SSL models. The proposed method is simple and could fit with a variety of SSL models. While the paper can be made better by comparing to more related ensembling baselines to guide practical use, the current presented empirical results is quite comprehensive on its own, and the usefulness of the proposed method is well-justified by the empirical results.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: Model ensembling is a common technology to further boost performance. While most paper study how to ensemble on supervised models, this paper focus on self-supervised models (SSL). Then, this paper discusses where to ensemble and how to ensemble the models training from SSL, proposing a downstream-efficient ensemble method based on weighted cross-entropy objectives. The results on few-shot learning such as imagenet-1k (1%) improve baselines of SSL method (DINO/MSN).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
With the development of SSL, it is necessary to study SSL model ensemble. The paper is rich in theoretical basis, Explaining clustering-based self-supervised learning methods from the perspective of maximum likelihood distinctly. This paper proposed a method that can be effective solutions to ensemble multi models, because of ensembling only applying to the head and codebook, not the encoder. This paper is easy to follow, and experiments are enough to support the claims made in this paper. 

Couple questions come to mind:

1.This method is effective but I think it's not enough to just ensemble the head and codebook, most researchers are probably more concerned how to ensemble the encoder part.

2.How extensible is this method? The method is verified in the double-branch structure (DINO/MSN) and can it be applied to the most popular MIM methods such as MAE. 

3.How to acquire multiple models to ensemble and how to ensure the diversity of the models, it seems to be less described in the paper.

4.If I am not wrong, the authors only fuse different heads, and how these different sub-models are obtained, from the intermediate checkpoints, and how can ensure the diversity of these heads. In my opinion, the diversity of the head counts for then ensemble, and how about choosing other heads from even smaller backbones but with large diversity. How is the method compared with another simple method model soups [1] that ensembles the encoders of different checkpoints and do not increase the inference time.  
Overall, although the results are impressive, I still wonder the rationale of the methods.
[1] Model soups: averaging weights of multiple fine-tuned models improves accuracy without increasing inference time

5. It is better to add average number in Table 3 for clarify.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This paper investigates how ensemble techniques, so successful in supervised learning, can be adapted to modern self-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks. The authors propose a “peri-training” ensemble mechanism that replicates only the non-representation components of an SSL model—namely, projection heads and codebooks—as opposed to the full encoder. Paired with a family of data-dependent weighted cross-entropy losses, this approach is designed to incur negligible cost during downstream evaluation (since only the main encoder is retained) while improving representation quality. The method is instantiated for two state-of-the-art clustering-based SSL methods, DINO and MSN, and demonstrates consistent gains across a variety of ImageNet-1K metrics, especially in the few-shot regime (e.g., 3.9 p.p. improvement over MSN ViT-B/16 for 1-shot learning).  

Strengths:  
1. Timely Topic. As SSL methods continue to push the frontier in label-efficient learning, exploring how well-known ensemble ideas carry over to SSL is a natural and overlooked direction. The paper brings renewed attention to a simple yet powerful idea—ensembling—in the context of unlabeled data.  
2. Theoretical Framing. The authors carefully cast clustering-based SSL objectives (SWaV, DINO, MSN) as approximations to maximum likelihood, which paves the way for formally deriving weighted ensemble losses (Sec. 2 and 3.2). This perspective unifies a variety of clustering and self-distillation methods under an MLE lens.  
3. Downstream Efficiency. By constraining ensembles to projection heads and codebooks—throw-away components often discarded at evaluation—the proposed method sidesteps any additional inference cost, a clear practical advantage over full-model ensembling.  
4. Empirical Rigor. The paper includes ablation studies on where to ensemble (head only, codebook only, or both) and how to weight member outputs (UNIF, PROB, ENT), verifying that entropy-based weighting (ENT) produces the most diverse and highest-performing ensembles (Table 1, Fig. 4). Results span k-NN, linear, and few-shot evaluations (1-, 2-, and 5-shot), and transfer learning to nine downstream datasets (Table 3).  

Weaknesses and Questions:  
1. Limitation to Head/Codebook Ensembles. The authors explicitly restrict their ensembles to projection heads and codebooks, leaving the main encoder untouched (Sec. 3.1). While this yields downstream-efficient ensembles, it also sidesteps the more fundamental question of whether ensembling the encoder itself could produce even larger gains. Many researchers may view head-only ensembling as a first step, but the manuscript provides no exploratory experiments or discussion on encoder-level ensembles. This leaves the reader wondering about the full potential of ensemble SSL.  
2. Extensibility to Masked Image Modeling (MIM). The experiments focus on clustering-based SSL methods (DINO, MSN). Yet masked autoencoders (MAE) and related MIM approaches have become extremely popular (Sec. 4). Although the authors suggest in the Conclusion that their framework might apply to MAE (by ensembling decoders or prediction heads, at increased cost), no empirical or even small-scale experiment is provided. Without such validation, it is difficult to gauge whether the method truly generalizes beyond two clustering-based algorithms.  
3. Origin and Diversity of Ensemble Members. The paper does not clearly articulate how the different projection heads and codebooks are initialized or diversified. Are all heads initialized independently from scratch (Sec. 3.1 suggests yes), or are some pulled from intermediate checkpoints to encourage complementary representations? Given the well-known importance of member diversity in ensemble performance, the reader would benefit from more detail on initialization schemes, or even a small study comparing random vs. checkpoint-based ensembles.  
4. Comparison to Model Soups. Wortsman et al. (2022) introduced “model soups,” which average weights of multiple fine-tuned models (often drawn from checkpoints along a single training run) to improve performance at no extra inference cost. This technique directly ensembles encoders rather than only heads, and requires no change to training code. The current manuscript does not include any empirical or conceptual comparison to model soups. How do peri-training head ensembles compare to post-training encoder soups in terms of accuracy, computational cost, and hyperparameter sensitivity?  
5. Need for Quantitative Averages in Table 3. Table 3 reports transfer accuracies on nine datasets for four methods (DINO∗, DINO∗-ENT, MSN∗, MSN∗-ENT) but omits the average column. Including the mean and standard deviation across datasets would clarify the overall transfer benefit while accounting for dataset-to-dataset variance.  

Additional Observations:  
– Temperature Sensitivity. Figure 6 shows that lowering the teacher temperature alone can improve few-shot performance for DINO∗, but ENT-based weighting still outperforms any fixed temperature baseline. This underscores that data-dependent specialization (ENT) is more effective than a simple global sharpening.  
– Ensemble Size and Diminishing Returns. Figure 3a indicates that performance gains plateau beyond 16 projection heads. A discussion of the trade-off between head count, training cost (Table 4), and marginal gains would help practitioners choose ensemble sizes in resource-constrained settings.  
– Diversity Measurement. The code alignment procedure (Hungarian algorithm) and subsequent similarity decay plots (Fig. 4, Sec. C.4) convincingly show that ENT fosters larger head diversity than UNIF or PROB. This solidifies the correlation between diversity and downstream performance.  

Summary of the Review:  
This manuscript brings an interesting and underexplored idea—head-only ensembling—to the SSL domain, complete with a solid theoretical framing and extensive empirical validation for DINO and MSN on ImageNet-1K and downstream tasks. However, it remains a preliminary study. Key questions regarding encoder ensembling, generalization to MIM methods, member initialization strategies, and comparisons to existing techniques such as model soups are left unanswered. These gaps weaken the manuscript’s impact and leave the reader uncertain about the method’s broader applicability.  

Rating:  
3: reject – not good enough

title: Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver for Graph Neural Networks with Equilibrium States

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing graph neural networks. Based on this perspective, the paper adopts two existing algorithms such as stochastic proximal gradient descent and its variance-reduced version to accelerate the forward computation with sampling. The backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed algorithm.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
# Strength:

The motivation and background of the studied problem have been clearly demonstrated, and the paper is well written. The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing GNNs. The finite-sum reformulation draws a natural connection with stochastic proximal gradient algorithm and the variance-reduced variant. The idea is simple but effective and efficient as demonstrated in the experiments. 

# Weakness

The forward computation of the solver is natural, simple, and well-justified. However, the backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. This seems surprising if it can work well in practice. It would be great if the author can provide solid theoretical justification as well as the empirical study on the gradient approximation error due to this approximation. Moreover, the idea of sampling is standard in stochastic optimization. Therefore, the contribution and novelty are a bit weak.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is clearly written with good quality. The idea is simple but effective.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper provides a simple but effective approach for the training of large-scale implicit GNNs. The effectiveness and efficiency are clearly demonstrated. Solid theoretical and empirical justification of the backward computation can further improve the paper.

## After rebuttal

The revision improves the paper. I am happy to increase my score.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This paper tackles the computational bottleneck in graph equilibrium models—specifically unfolded GNNs and implicit GNNs—by reframing their forward pass as a finite-sum optimization problem.  Building on the connection between graph denoising objectives and equilibrium states, the authors propose two solvers: the Unbiased Stochastic Proximal solver (USP) and its variance-reduced variant (USP-VR).  USP samples subgraphs at each iteration and applies a stochastic proximal gradient step (Eqns. (5)–(6)), while USP-VR further incorporates variance reduction (Eqn. (8)) to achieve a linear convergence rate (Proposition 2).  The authors prove in Proposition 1 that USP is unbiased in expectation and converges sub-linearly to the same equilibrium solution obtained by a full-graph deterministic solver, and that USP-VR attains linear convergence.  On datasets ranging from PPI and Reddit to OGBN-Arxiv and Products, USP and USP-VR consistently match or exceed the accuracy of baselines (APPNP, IGNN) while delivering 2×–10× speedups in forward and backward passes.  Similar efficiency gains are also shown on graph classification benchmarks (D&D, COLLAB).

Strengths:  
1. Motivation and Background.  The paper provides a clear and concise exposition of why standard fixed-point or gradient-descent solvers for graph equilibrium models scale poorly on large or dense graphs.  By relating equilibrium GNNs (IGNN, APPNP, IRLS) to graph denoising objectives (Eqn. (2)) and then to a finite-sum form over edges (Eqn. (3)–(4)), the authors build a strong optimization-perspective foundation.  

2. Theoretical Guarantees.  The proposed USP solver enjoys an unbiased gradient estimate (Proposition 1: E[∇g_{\hat E_k}(Z)] = ∇g(Z)) and sub-linear convergence O(1/t) in expectation.  USP-VR further reduces variance via periodic full-graph corrections, yielding a linear convergence factor ρ < 1 under standard Lipschitz and strong-convexity assumptions (Proposition 2).  These proofs are nontrivial extensions of classical proximal stochastic gradient analyses to the graph setting, and they give confidence that the outputs match those of the vanilla solvers in expectation.

3. Empirical Effectiveness and Efficiency.  Extensive experiments on six large-scale node classification datasets (Flickr, Reddit, Yelp, PPI, OGBN-Arxiv, OGBN-Products) demonstrate that both USP and USP-VR solvers achieve state-of-the-art performance.  For instance, on Reddit, IGNN+USP-VR attains 98.5% accuracy—comparable to the full solver—while cutting forward-pass time by >10×.  On OGBN-Products, APPNP+USP yields a 6× speedup with a small 0.2% accuracy gain.  Graph classification results on D&D and COLLAB similarly confirm 1.5×–3× speedups with no loss in classification quality.  The authors also provide convergence curves (Figure 1) to illustrate USP’s rapid initial descent and USP-VR’s superior final convergence.

4. Clarity and Reproducibility.  The paper is well-organized and clearly written.  Key algorithmic steps are laid out in Algorithm 1 and Eqns. (5)–(9).  Implementation details (choice of m, m₂, step sizes, one-step Neumann approximation in backpropagation) are thoroughly documented.  The codebase is based on standard frameworks (PyTorch, PyG, DGL) and should be easy to reproduce.

Weaknesses:  
1. Backward Pass Approximation.  While the forward stochastic proximal steps are well backed by theory, the backward computation resorts to a one-step Neumann gradient approximation (Eqn. (9)) around the equilibrium point.  This shortcut is “surprisingly” effective in practice, but the paper lacks a formal analysis or empirical study quantifying the bias introduced by this approximation.  A deeper theoretical justification—perhaps bounding the error ‖∂L/∂θ_exact − ∂L/∂θ_approx‖—or an ablation comparing full implicit differentiation versus the one-step update on smaller graphs would strengthen confidence in this design choice.

2. Contribution and Novelty.  The idea of sampling mini-batches or subgraphs to accelerate GNN training is well-explored in stochastic optimization literature (e.g., GraphSAGE, FastGCN, VR-GCN).  Although this work is the first, to my knowledge, to apply stochastic proximal and variance-reduced proximal solvers specifically to graph equilibrium models, the core sampling strategy and the use of known stochastic optimization algorithms are fairly standard.  A more in-depth discussion of what unique challenges the graph equilibrium setting poses (beyond computational cost) and how the solvers specifically overcome these (e.g., handling non-differentiable activations via proximal steps) would clarify the novelty.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:  
Overall, the manuscript is of high clarity and quality.  The finite-sum reformulation (Eqn. (3)–(4)) and the connection to proximal gradient methods are insightful, and the empirical evaluation is comprehensive.  However, as noted, the sampling idea is fairly standard, and the backward gradient approximation warrants additional theoretical or experimental support.

Summary of the Review:  
This paper delivers a compelling and well‒executed approach to speeding up large-scale implicit GNNs by importing unbiased stochastic proximal algorithms and a variance-reduced variant.  Theoretical analyses provide unbiasedness and convergence rates, and experiments across node and graph classification tasks exhibit strong performance and significant speed gains.  Addressing the backward-pass approximation more rigorously would further enhance the work, but even as is, the contribution marks meaningful progress in scalable GNN training.  Following the authors’ rebuttal and revisions, I am happy to maintain a rating of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.  

Rating (6): marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing graph neural networks. Based on this perspective, the paper adopts two existing algorithms such as stochastic proximal gradient descent and its variance-reduced version to accelerate the forward computation with sampling. The backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed algorithm.

Strength And Weaknesses:
# Strength:

The motivation and background of the studied problem have been clearly demonstrated, and the paper is well written. The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing GNNs. The finite-sum reformulation draws a natural connection with stochastic proximal gradient algorithm and the variance-reduced variant. The idea is simple but effective and efficient as demonstrated in the experiments. 

# Weakness

The forward computation of the solver is natural, simple, and well-justified. However, the backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. This seems surprising if it can work well in practice. It would be great if the author can provide solid theoretical justification as well as the empirical study on the gradient approximation error due to this approximation. Moreover, the idea of sampling is standard in stochastic optimization. Therefore, the contribution and novelty are a bit weak.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is clearly written with good quality. The idea is simple but effective.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper provides a simple but effective approach for the training of large-scale implicit GNNs. The effectiveness and efficiency are clearly demonstrated. Solid theoretical and empirical justification of the backward computation can further improve the paper.

## After rebuttal

The revision improves the paper. I am happy to increase my score.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: Two unbiased stochastic proximal solvers for learning graph equilibrium models are proposed. They are inspired by the stochastic proximal gradient descent method and its variance reduction variant (called USP and USP-VR solver). Both provide considerable computational speed-ups in comparison with the original solvers.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
+ A simple, computationally less expensive to optimize graph equilibrium models is proposed.
+ The authors provide convergence guarantees of the average proposed sampling method and derive its convergence rate (O(1/t))
+ They also propose a simple variance reduction scheme that enjoys a linear convergent rate like the vanilla deterministic solvers.

Weaknesses and open questions:
- The claimed performance improvements in Table 2 are often not significant.
- How does the run time (in Figure 2) compare to explicit methods (which often do not perform much worse).
- The equilibrium solution $Z^*$ needs to be defined precisely. Currently, the formulation of Proposition 1 suggests that $Z^*$ might be unique, which is not the case in general. Also the experiments confirm this, since the IGNN variants do not seem to converge to the same solutions.
- How is the proposal different from (Chen et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2017) for "traditional graph models" and why should these not transfer to graph equilibrium models?
- The convergence results in expectation seem trivial as, in expectation, the procedure is not different from optimising the full model. The real challenge would lie in the analysis of the error that is introduced by the sampling scheme (or the average number of additional SGD steps to reach an equilibrium that are required for the actual sampling scheme and not its average).
- A complexity analysis of the proposed algorithm would strengthen the claim of computational advantages.

Points of minor critique and open questions:
- Examples for f on page 3 would be appreciated.
- Table 3 does not report any significance intervals.
- The formulation is restricted to symmetric weight structure $WW^T$ but the authors claim that this has no effect on the performance of the model. (It should impact its expressive power though...)
- In contrast to what the authors claim the weight normalisation will influence the training dynamics.
- page 5/6: `Therefore, our USP-VR solver can achieve a more accurate solution compared with the USP-VR solver with limited propagation times in the forward procedure.' does not seem to make sense.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The paper does not make a very polished impression and is for that reason hard to follow from time to time.
It should be discussed more concretely why previous cannot be transferred to the setting of equilibrium because this does not seem to be apparent.
The proposed scheme seems relatively trivial but this can also be a feature.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
A strength of the paper is that the methodological proposals achieve performance speed-ups that are backed up by theoretical investigations.
It is not sufficiently clear to me, however, whether the contribution is sufficiently novel in comparison with similar schemes that have been developed for "non-equilibrium" models.
Furthermore, the presentation of the paper could be improved.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
Two unbiased stochastic proximal solvers for learning graph equilibrium models are proposed. They are inspired by the stochastic proximal gradient descent method and its variance reduction variant (called USP and USP-VR solver). Both provide considerable computational speed-ups in comparison with the original solvers.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
+ A simple, computationally less expensive to optimize graph equilibrium models is proposed.
+ The authors provide convergence guarantees of the average proposed sampling method and derive its convergence rate (O(1/t))
+ They also propose a simple variance reduction scheme that enjoys a linear convergent rate like the vanilla deterministic solvers.

Weaknesses and open questions:
- The claimed performance improvements in Table 2 are often not significant.
- How does the run time (in Figure 2) compare to explicit methods (which often do not perform much worse).
- The equilibrium solution $Z^*$ needs to be defined precisely. Currently, the formulation of Proposition 1 suggests that $Z^*$ might be unique, which is not the case in general. Also the experiments confirm this, since the IGNN variants do not seem to converge to the same solutions.
- How is the proposal different from (Chen et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2017) for "traditional graph models" and why should these not transfer to graph equilibrium models?
- The convergence results in expectation seem trivial as, in expectation, the procedure is not different from optimising the full model. The real challenge would lie in the analysis of the error that is introduced by the sampling scheme (or the average number of additional SGD steps to reach an equilibrium that are required for the actual sampling scheme and not its average).
- A complexity analysis of the proposed algorithm would strengthen the claim of computational advantages.

Points of minor critique and open questions:
- Examples for f on page 3 would be appreciated.
- Table 3 does not report any significance intervals.
- The formulation is restricted to symmetric weight structure $WW^T$ but the authors claim that this has no effect on the performance of the model. (It should impact its expressive power though...)
- In contrast to what the authors claim the weight normalisation will influence the training dynamics.
- page 5/6: `Therefore, our USP-VR solver can achieve a more accurate solution compared with the USP-VR solver with limited propagation times in the forward procedure.' does not seem to make sense.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper does not make a very polished impression and is for that reason hard to follow from time to time.
It should be discussed more concretely why previous cannot be transferred to the setting of equilibrium because this does not seem to be apparent.
The proposed scheme seems relatively trivial but this can also be a feature.

Summary Of The Review:
A strength of the paper is that the methodological proposals achieve performance speed-ups that are backed up by theoretical investigations.
It is not sufficiently clear to me, however, whether the contribution is sufficiently novel in comparison with similar schemes that have been developed for "non-equilibrium" models.
Furthermore, the presentation of the paper could be improved.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposed an efficient way to compute Graph Equilibrium models. The computation of the Graph Equilibrium model is an optimization problem of a graph denoising problem, and its naive solution is computationally expensive due to the use of an entire graph. This paper introduced the Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver (USP-solver) and its variance-reduction version USP-VR. They compute the Graph Equilibrium model by sampling the edges of the underlying graph. It was shown that USP converges to the equilibrium state at a sub-linear rate and USP-VR at a linear rate. Numerical experiments were conducted on node prediction and graph prediction problems to verify the practical usefulness of the proposed methods.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed methods have guarantees for convergence.
- Numerical experiments demonstrated the usefulness of the proposed methods on large graph datasets.

Weaknesses
- Novelty in methodology is somewhat limited because randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches to improve optimization algorithms.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity

It took me some time to understand the method on first reading because (1) looks like one layer of an ordinal GNN, although the main interest of this paper is implicit GNNs. Therefore, I would suggest briefly writing the formulation of implicit GNNs, and how (1) is derived.
Nevertheless, overall, the paper is well-written.


Quality

The proposed methods are justified from both theoretical and empirical points of view. First, Propositions 1 and 2 ensure that the proposed methods converge to an equilibrium state, demonstrating their theoretical soundness. Regarding the empirical evaluations, I want to clarify what the authors meant by computational complexity (e.g., in the abstract). Figure 2, Table 3, and Figure 3 certainly provide evidence for the improvement of numerical improvements in computational speed. They have also shown that there is no degradation in prediction performance. If the authors also intended space complexity, it would be desirable to evaluate memory usage quantitatively. That said, memory efficiency is implicitly evaluated, although I am not sure the authors intended this. For example, in Table 2, when using the Reddit dataset, the vanilla IGNNs caused OOM, while IGNN+USP and IGNN+USP-VR did not. 

Novelty

If my understanding is correct, randomization is a relatively standard approach to reduce the computational complexity of optimization algorithms in expectation or with high probability. Also, variance reduction is a standard technique. In this sense, the method proposed in this paper is a natural improvement of the algorithm.

Reproducibility

The Appendix described the hyperparameters of the prediction model. Although this paper did not describe every full detail of dataset preparations, this is not a problem because references and dataset specifications are available. Since code is not provided, there is no guarantee of perfect reproduction. However, I think we can implement the code to reproduce the experiments to some degree.

Minor Comments

- P3 (1): $Z^{(n)}$, $Z^{(n+1)}$, $Z$ are undefined.
- P4 $\|WW^\top\| \leq 1/\tilde{A}$: this formula is invalid because the left hand side is scaler-valued, while the right hand side is vector-valued.
- P4 (3): $g(Z) + f(Z)$ -> $\min_{Z} g(Z) + f(Z)$
- P6 last equation: $\sum_{(k1, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$ -> $\sum_{(k_11, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Although randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches, as far as I know, these approaches have no application to implicit GNNs. In addition, the method is sound in that it has theoretical guarantees, and numerical experiments have shown its practical usefulness.

# Post-rebuttal comments

The responses by the authors solved my questions. Therefore, I want to keep my score, leaning to accept.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposed an efficient way to compute Graph Equilibrium models. The computation of the Graph Equilibrium model is an optimization problem of a graph denoising problem, and its naive solution is computationally expensive due to the use of an entire graph. This paper introduced the Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver (USP-solver) and its variance-reduction version USP-VR. They compute the Graph Equilibrium model by sampling the edges of the underlying graph. It was shown that USP converges to the equilibrium state at a sub-linear rate and USP-VR at a linear rate. Numerical experiments were conducted on node prediction and graph prediction problems to verify the practical usefulness of the proposed methods.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed methods have guarantees for convergence.
- Numerical experiments demonstrated the usefulness of the proposed methods on large graph datasets.

Weaknesses
- Novelty in methodology is somewhat limited because randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches to improve optimization algorithms.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity

It took me some time to understand the method on first reading because (1) looks like one layer of an ordinal GNN, although the main interest of this paper is implicit GNNs. Therefore, I would suggest briefly writing the formulation of implicit GNNs, and how (1) is derived.
Nevertheless, overall, the paper is well-written.


Quality

The proposed methods are justified from both theoretical and empirical points of view. First, Propositions 1 and 2 ensure that the proposed methods converge to an equilibrium state, demonstrating their theoretical soundness. Regarding the empirical evaluations, I want to clarify what the authors meant by computational complexity (e.g., in the abstract). Figure 2, Table 3, and Figure 3 certainly provide evidence for the improvement of numerical improvements in computational speed. They have also shown that there is no degradation in prediction performance. If the authors also intended space complexity, it would be desirable to evaluate memory usage quantitatively. That said, memory efficiency is implicitly evaluated, although I am not sure the authors intended this. For example, in Table 2, when using the Reddit dataset, the vanilla IGNNs caused OOM, while IGNN+USP and IGNN+USP-VR did not. 

Novelty

If my understanding is correct, randomization is a relatively standard approach to reduce the computational complexity of optimization algorithms in expectation or with high probability. Also, variance reduction is a standard technique. In this sense, the method proposed in this paper is a natural improvement of the algorithm.

Reproducibility

The Appendix described the hyperparameters of the prediction model. Although this paper did not describe every full detail of dataset preparations, this is not a problem because references and dataset specifications are available. Since code is not provided, there is no guarantee of perfect reproduction. However, I think we can implement the code to reproduce the experiments to some degree.

Minor Comments

- P3 (1): $Z^{(n)}$, $Z^{(n+1)}$, $Z$ are undefined.
- P4 $\|WW^\top\| \leq 1/\tilde{A}$: this formula is invalid because the left hand side is scaler-valued, while the right hand side is vector-valued.
- P4 (3): $g(Z) + f(Z)$ -> $\min_{Z} g(Z) + f(Z)$
- P6 last equation: $\sum_{(k1, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$ -> $\sum_{(k_11, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$

Summary Of The Review:
Although randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches, as far as I know, these approaches have no application to implicit GNNs. In addition, the method is sound in that it has theoretical guarantees, and numerical experiments have shown its practical usefulness.

# Post-rebuttal comments

The responses by the authors solved my questions. Therefore, I want to keep my score, leaning to accept.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
The manuscript tackles the high computational cost of implicit and unfolded Graph Neural Networks (IGNNs and related “graph equilibrium” models) by reframing their forward pass as a finite‐sum optimization problem and then applying unbiased stochastic proximal solvers. Specifically, the authors introduce two algorithms—USP (Unbiased Stochastic Proximal) and USP‐VR (with variance reduction)—that randomly split the edge set into subgraphs and perform proximal gradient updates on sampled subsets. They provide theoretical guarantees showing these solvers converge in expectation to the same equilibrium as the full‐graph deterministic methods, and they demonstrate through experiments on large benchmarks (e.g., Reddit, OGBN‐Arxiv, OGBN‐Product) that their approach can achieve comparable or even slightly better node‐classification accuracy while speeding up propagation by factors of 2×–10× versus vanilla IGNN or APPNP.

Strengths:  
1. The authors propose a method to accelerate the IGNN.  
   – By interpreting the IGNN forward pass as solving a graph denoising problem and decomposing it into edge‐based sub‐objectives (Eqn. 3 and 4), the work leverages well‐studied stochastic proximal gradient and variance‐reduced updates (Eqs. 5–8).  
   – The theoretical analysis (Propositions 1 and 2) is clearly laid out: USP yields sublinear convergence in expectation, while USP‐VR enjoys a linear rate under standard Lipschitz and strong convexity assumptions.  
   – Empirical results on six large node‐classification datasets and two graph‐classification benchmarks validate the computational savings (up to 10× faster on dense graphs like Reddit) without degrading accuracy (Tables 2 and 3, Figures 2 and 3).  
   – The paper is generally well‐written and provides sufficient details to reproduce the experiments, including parameter settings (m, m2, hidden dimensions), hardware (RTX‐3090), and software frameworks (PyTorch + PyG + DGL).

Weaknesses:  
1. This work is incremental—it only works for some specific frameworks (here IGNN).  
   – Although the paper frames its solvers abstractly for “graph equilibrium” models, all derivations, assumptions (e.g., symmetric WW⊤, Lipschitz constants L in (1,2)), and experiments center on IGNN and APPNP instances. The applicability to continuous‐depth models such as ODE‐based GNNs or PDE‐inspired graph networks remains unexplored.  
   – The use of subgraph sampling and variance reduction is a straightforward adaptation of existing stochastic proximal methods (e.g., Xiao & Zhang 2014), so the technical novelty in the optimization component is limited.  
   – If each specialized GNN were to receive its own “stochastic solver” plugin, the contributions become more like engineering wrappers rather than a unifying theoretical advance for the broader GNN community. It is unclear whether this line of work will inspire new paradigms beyond incremental speed‐ups.  
   – No ablation is reported for how sensitive the method is to the choice of m (number of subgraphs) and m2 (VR update frequency) beyond Reddit; such hyperparameters can significantly affect both convergence rate and variance. A more thorough study of these trade‐offs would strengthen the claims.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:  
– Clarity and Quality: The overall presentation is coherent, with clear notation (e.g., Eqns. 1–3), algorithm pseudocode (Algorithm 1), and well‐organized experiments. The discussion of related work situates the paper within optimization‐based GNN analyses (Zhu et al. 2021; Yang et al. 2021b).  
– Reproducibility: Key hyperparameters and data splits are provided in the Appendix; code release would further solidify reproducibility.  
– Novelty: While the convergence proofs for USP/USP‐VR applied to graph‐equilibrium objectives are a useful exercise, they largely mirror known results in the proximal stochastic gradient literature. The methodological innovation, beyond plugging established VR techniques into graph solvers, is modest.  
– Impact: The solver achieves meaningful speed‐ups for IGNN and APPNP, but its insight does not readily generalize to other classes of deep graph architectures, such as continuous-time ODE‐GNNs or diffusion PDE approaches (Xu et al. 2020a; Chamberlain et al. 2021).

Additional Observations and Suggestions:  
– It would be interesting to see whether the USP‐VR framework could extend to dynamic graphs or to models with attention mechanisms, since the current assumptions restrict to static, symmetric‐weight GNNs.  
– The comparison in Section 4.4 to GraphSAINT and SAGE‐based sampling is helpful, but a more detailed runtime breakdown (aggregation vs. linear layers vs. data loading) would clarify where the speed‐up arises.  
– Discussion of memory usage—especially for the variance‐reduced solver, which stores a full‐graph gradient checkpoint every m2 steps—would help practitioners assess the trade‐offs on GPU‐constrained hardware.  
– The paper might benefit from a short paragraph in the Conclusion on potential future directions, such as unifying this approach with other GNN acceleration strategies or applying it to richer modeling domains (e.g., spatio‐temporal graphs).

Summary of the Review:  
In summary, this paper delivers a targeted acceleration for IGNN‐style graph equilibrium models via unbiased stochastic proximal updates and variance reduction. It is clearly written and empirically effective, but the contribution is incremental, scoped narrowly to a particular GNN class, and relies on well‐known optimization techniques. The novelty is therefore limited, and I am personally inclined to reject this paper. Of course, I welcome further discussion and other perspectives before finalizing this decision.

Rating:  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary Of The Paper:
This work introduces the sampling way in the GNNs from optimization perspective. And thus it alleviates the computation cost on graph diffusion in different iterations. Furthermore, Variance Reduction (VR) is introduced for improving the performance.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. The authors propose a method to accelerate the IGNN.

Weakness:
1. This work is incremental it only works for some specific frameworks (here is IGNN). However, there are so many GNNs like ODE and PDE GNN. If this type of incremental work makes it into the ICLR, we should have many for different GNNs in future. But does it inspire any member in this community? Is this really necessary?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The whole paper is good, both in terms of clarity and quality. But the novelty is trivial in my opinion.

Summary Of The Review:
According to my comments above, I am personally inclined to reject this paper. Of course I am happy to see other different opinions before making a decision.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Learning Adversarial Linear Mixture Markov Decision Processes with Bandit Feedback and Unknown Transition

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers the adversarial linear adversarial MDP and proposes the first efficient algorithm that achieves $\sqrt{K}$ bound. Moreover, they also give a lower bound. Their techniques are based on the occupancy measurements which is initially proposed by Jin et al. (2020b). Compared to previous techniques, this paper adopts some nontrivial extensions, the main one, as stated in the paper, is the use of a carefully chosen " imaginary next state", which solves the dependence between the actual next states and thus allows the usage of regular linear regression to estimate the $\theta$
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength: The results are significant. The techniques seem novel to me -- (1) the use of " imaginary next state" is novel (2) the further improvement on H, S compared to Jin et al. (2020b) seems also interesting (but it is not very clearly stated)

Weakness: Some of the technique contributions are not clearly stated in the paper.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The paper is overall well-written and easy to read. But as I mentioned above, I feel there are some technical novelties that are not clearly explained in the main paper. Specifically,

1\ When talking about how to choose the next state in Eqn.(2), the author also mentioned: " for some technical reasons". But from my perspective it is not that trivial, my understanding is that it is like some "implicit exploration", choosing to maximize the most uncertain states? I would like to see a little more discussion on that.

2\ In remark 2, you said that "it is not a straightforward extension, even given the transition estimator obtained using the imaginary next-states." So my understanding is that, besides the imaginary next-states techniques, there are some other techniques that help you to further shave the dependency. I would like to see some discussion in the main paper.

3\ I personally think section 4.2 can be more concise since it basically gives a summary of the existing standard techniques.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers the adversarial linear adversarial MDP and proposes the first efficient algorithm that achieves $\sqrt{K}$ bound. Moreover, they also give a lower bound. Their techniques are based on the occupancy measurements which is initially proposed by Jin et al. (2020b). Compared to previous techniques, this paper adopts some nontrivial extensions, the main one, as stated in the paper, is the use of a carefully chosen " imaginary next state", which solves the dependence between the actual next states and thus allows the usage of regular linear regression to estimate the $\theta$

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength: The results are significant. The techniques seem novel to me -- (1) the use of " imaginary next state" is novel (2) the further improvement on H, S compared to Jin et al. (2020b) seems also interesting (but it is not very clearly stated)

Weakness: Some of the technique contributions are not clearly stated in the paper.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is overall well-written and easy to read. But as I mentioned above, I feel there are some technical novelties that are not clearly explained in the main paper. Specifically,

1\ When talking about how to choose the next state in Eqn.(2), the author also mentioned: " for some technical reasons". But from my perspective it is not that trivial, my understanding is that it is like some "implicit exploration", choosing to maximize the most uncertain states? I would like to see a little more discussion on that.

2\ In remark 2, you said that "it is not a straightforward extension, even given the transition estimator obtained using the imaginary next-states." So my understanding is that, besides the imaginary next-states techniques, there are some other techniques that help you to further shave the dependency. I would like to see some discussion in the main paper.

3\ I personally think section 4.2 can be more concise since it basically gives a summary of the existing standard techniques.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall I think it has good results and relatively novel techniques. But the explanation of some techniques seems not very clear to me. So I give 6 and would like to raise my score if I can better understand the novelty of the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition and bandit feedback. The authors propose an algorithm with an upper bound $\tilde{O}(dS^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$, and prove an almost near-matching lower bound (in $d, K, A$). The authors highlight the use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma for linear mixture MDPs, which might be of independent interest.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

(+) The upper bound is accompanied by a near-matching lower bound. The authors also justify the dependence on $S$ and $A$ via the lower bound.

(+) The use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma (Appendix B.1).

Weaknesses:

(-) The bound depends on $S$, which is unusual and undesirable given the linear mixture MDPs assumption.

(-) The writing can be improved, especially the main results.

Detailed comments:

- I am familiar with statistical RL theory, but not so much in the adversarial setting. Hence, I will comment more on the writing/correctness aspects of the paper rather than the novelty/contribution.

- (main concern) Since the upper bound in Theorem 1 has a dependence on both $S$ and $A$, would this be just the tabular setting? I don’t quite understand where the linear approximation comes in when we still have the dependence on $S$.

- I encourage the authors to make it more explicit which elements are novel, particularly in your algorithm design. For example, how does your proposed algorithm differ from Jin et al. (2020b)?

- The main results (Theorem 1 and 2) should be made more detailed and self-contained. I encourage the authors to at least briefly introduce the settings and various relevant notations in the Theorems.

- The lower bound contains a term $dH\sqrt{K}$, which is linear in the horizon $H$. However, the upper bound only has a $\sqrt{H}$ dependence. Would this lead to a contradiction, or am I missing something here?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The literature review and comparison with existing works are sufficient.

Novelty: The analysis in the paper is sufficiently novel. The analysis utilizes a novel occupancy measure difference (Lemma 2), which is a non-trivial extension from Jin et al. (2020b). Although the tightness of Lemma 2 is unknown, it may be of independent interest and useful for the community.  

Summary Of The Review: The paper studies adversarially linear mixture MDPs. The main results are interesting with a relatively novel analysis. However, the implications of the main results are unclear: what did we gain from the linear MDPs assumptions if the upper bound still depends on $S$?

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition and bandit feedback. The authors propose an algorithm with an upper bound $\tilde{O}(dS^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$, and prove an almost near-matching lower bound (in $d, K, A$). The authors highlight the use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma for linear mixture MDPs, which might be of independent interest.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

(+) The upper bound is accompanied by a near-matching lower bound. The authors also justify the dependence on $S$ and $A$ via the lower bound.

(+) The use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma (Appendix B.1).

Weaknesses:

(-) The bound depends on $S$, which is unusual and undesirable given the linear mixture MDPs assumption.

(-) The writing can be improved, especially the main results.

Detailed comments:

- I am familiar with statistical RL theory, but not so much in the adversarial setting. Hence, I will comment more on the writing/correctness aspects of the paper rather than the novelty/contribution.

- (main concern) Since the upper bound in Theorem 1 has a dependence on both $S$ and $A$, would this be just the tabular setting? I don’t quite understand where the linear approximation comes in when we still have the dependence on $S$.

- I encourage the authors to make it more explicit which elements are novel, particularly in your algorithm design. For example, how does your proposed algorithm differ from Jin et al. (2020b)?

- The main results (Theorem 1 and 2) should be made more detailed and self-contained. I encourage the authors to at least briefly introduce the settings and various relevant notations in the Theorems.

- The lower bound contains a term $dH\sqrt{K}$, which is linear in the horizon $H$. However, the upper bound only has a $\sqrt{H}$ dependence. Would this lead to a contradiction, or am I missing something here?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The literature review and comparison with existing works are sufficient.

Novelty: The analysis in the paper is sufficiently novel. The analysis utilizes a novel occupancy measure difference (Lemma 2), which is a non-trivial extension from Jin et al. (2020b). Although the tightness of Lemma 2 is unknown, it may be of independent interest and useful for the community.  

Summary Of The Review: The paper studies adversarially linear mixture MDPs. The main results are interesting with a relatively novel analysis. However, the implications of the main results are unclear: what did we gain from the linear MDPs assumptions if the upper bound still depends on $S$?

The paper studies adversarially linear mixture MDPs. The main results are interesting with a relatively novel analysis. However, there is little innovation in the algorithm design, and the implications of the main results are unclear: what did we gain from the linear MDPs assumptions if the upper bound still depends on $S$?

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies inhomogeneous adversarial linear mixture MDPs. It presents an efficient algorithm called LSUOB-REPS which provably achieves $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d S^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$. The algorithm relies on occupancy measure based techniques, which maintains a confidence set of the unknown transition function and runs online mirror descent over the space of occupancy measures. One novelty is the construction of a specific linear regression objective function to solve for the transition parameter. To this end, this paper proposes to use an imaginary next state to construct the regression target. Furthermore, a lower bound of order $\Omega(dH\sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$ is proven, which shows that the algorithm has close-to-optimal performance.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

1. This paper gives the first answer to a quite challenging problem, i.e., the adversarial linear MDPs. Both an upper bound and a lower bound (under certain assumptions) are given.

2. The paper is very well-written. This is a very technical paper. Techniques are presented with rather detailed explanations, making it easier for the readers to follow.

3. The paper has some novelty. The technical challenges are also discussed quite thoroughly, showing the technical contributions of the paper clear. For example, the imaginary next-state seems like a quite interesting idea to deal with the challenge of constructing a confidence ellipsoid when the classical self-normalized concentration technique cannot be directly applied. 

---
Weakness: 

There is no major weakness as far as I can tell. There are some comments though. 
1. The dependence of the upper bound on $|S|$ is unpleasant under the linear mixture MDP setting. 

2. The paper lacks a very detailed comparison with [1]. Although it seems that [1] studies tabular MDPs which is different from linear mixture MDP, the major idea of algorithmic design is very similar. Furthermore, in the case of representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture MDP, we would have a relation $d = |S|^2 |A|$. In this case the bound in this paper would actually be worse than that of $1$ (but I can understand this since representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture is inefficient. Still a more detailed comparison would be good to have).

3. There is no discussion on computational efficiency of the algorithm, so it might be inappropriate to call the algorithm ‘efficient’.    

[1] Jin, Chi, et al. "Learning adversarial markov decision processes with bandit feedback and unknown transition." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: This paper is clear in general.Theorems have no ambiguity to me. Technical challenges and the corresponding solutions are described clearly. Related work is also discussed properly with now ambiguity. 


Quality: The quality is good. This is a purely theoretical paper and the theorems are solid. The proof feels correct to me, though I did not check all the details. 

Novelty: The paper is novel. The technical contributions, e.g. how to construct the confidence set of the transition parameter in the adversarial setting; how to choose the imaginary next state; are all good ideas in my opinion.


Reproducibility: This is purely theoretical work with no empirical result. The proof looks reproducible to me as it is written clearly and not very hard to read.

1. Solid contribution: this paper gives the answer to a quite challenging and important field of reinforcement learning theory. 

2. Some novelty: to solve certain technical challenges, some novel ideas have been proposed.

reviews.text:
Review for “Learning Adversarial Linear Mixture Markov Decision Processes with Bandit Feedback and Unknown Transition”  

Paper summary  
This paper tackles the challenging setting of episodic Markov decision processes (MDPs) where the transition kernel is a linear mixture model, the loss functions are chosen adversarially, and the learner only receives bandit feedback. In particular, the transition model satisfies Ph(s′|s,a)=⟨ϕ(s′|s,a),θ*h⟩ for some unknown θ*h, and the adversary picks a fresh loss ℓk∶S×A→[0,1] each episode. The authors propose LSUOB-REPS, an occupancy-measure algorithm that (1) builds a least-squares estimator of θ*h via an “imaginary next‐state” regression target (Lemma 1), (2) maintains an ellipsoid confidence set Pk for the transition, (3) constructs an optimistically biased loss estimator using an upper occupancy bound uk(s,a) (Section 4.2), and (4) updates its occupancy measure by online mirror descent over ∆(Pk). They prove a high-probability regret upper bound of Õ(d S²√K + √(HSAK)) (Theorem 1) and show a nearly matching lower bound Ω(d H√K + √(HSAK)) (Theorem 2). To my knowledge, this is the first sublinear regret result for adversarial linear-mixture MDPs under unknown transition and bandit feedback.

Strengths  

1. Novel algorithmic ideas.  
   – The use of an imaginary next-state s′k,h+1 chosen to maximize the uncertainty norm ∥ϕ(s′|sk,h,ak,h)∥M⁻¹ₖ₋₁,h (Eq. 2) is a clever twist on standard least-squares regression in linear mixture MDPs. This innovation allows control of the occupancy measure estimation error (Lemma 2), which is the main barrier in adversarial bandit feedback settings.  
   – Integrating implicit exploration (γ-shifted loss estimator via uk(s,a)+γ) with OCC-REPS style OMD over confidence sets elegantly handles both unknown transitions and adversarial losses, combining ideas from Jin et al. (2020a) and Rosenberg & Mansour (2019a).  

2. Rigorous theoretical guarantees.  
   – The upper bound Õ(d S²√K + √(HSAK)) is derived by carefully decomposing the regret into REG, ERROR, BIAS1, and BIAS2 terms (proof sketch in Section 5.1). The analysis handles the complicated dependency between the occupancy measure and the linear mixture transition in a nontrivial way (Appendix C).  
   – The lower bound of Ω(d H√K + √(HSAK))—obtained by reducing to parallel stochastic linear bandits in the first H/2 layers and to a combinatorial bandit in the last H/2 layers—demonstrates that the dependence on d, H, and √K is unavoidable (Section 5.2).  

3. Clarity and thorough discussion of technical challenges.  
   – The paper clearly contrasts the proposed transition regression target—using δs′(·) instead of the usual value‐targeted regression—highlighting why classical self-normalization cannot yield an ellipsoid when using value‐based targets (Section 4.1).  
   – Table 1 provides an insightful comparison of regret rates across tabular and linear MDPs, adversarial vs. stochastic losses, and full‐info vs. bandit feedback.  
   – Each novel idea is motivated by a precise technical obstacle, making the contributions transparent even in this highly technical work.

Weaknesses and suggestions  

1. Strong dependence on |S| in the regret bound.  
   – The upper bound scales as S², which is worse than the S-independent bound in the full-information case (He et al., 2022). The S² factor arises in Lemma 2 when controlling the difference between the estimated occupancy (q̂k) and the true occupancy (qk). It would be helpful to discuss whether stronger structural assumptions (e.g., MDP homogeneity or low-rank features on S×A) could mitigate this dependence, or if a refined analysis of the state‐action visitation probabilities could reduce it.  

2. Comparison with the tabular‐MDP algorithm of Jin et al. (2020a) is brief.  
   – While the paper acknowledges the similarity in OMD over occupancy sets, it could be illuminating to spell out explicitly how tabular UC-O-REPS (with d=SA) corresponds to a degenerate linear mixture instance with d=|S|²|A|. In that extreme encoding, the bound here becomes O(|S|⁴|A| √K), which is vacuous. A more detailed discussion of this translation and its inefficiencies would clarify the conceptual gap between tabular and feature-based methods.  

3. Computational complexity and “efficiency” claim.  
   – The term “efficient” is used to describe the algorithm, yet solving the OMD update over ∆(Pk+1) (Eq. 4) requires, in principle, access to a separation oracle for the confidence set of transition functions and repeated calls to COMP-UOB. It would strengthen the paper to outline the per-episode time complexity, e.g., the cost of updating Mk,h and bk,h (O(d²)), computing uk via dynamic programming (polynomial in S, A, H), and solving the mirror–descent subproblem. If any of these steps can be implemented in polynomial time (in d, S, A, H, log K), that should be emphasized.  

Minor comments / possible extensions  

• Empirical evaluation. Even a small-scale synthetic experiment could illustrate how the S² term affects performance in practice, and verify that the imaginary next‐state design indeed stabilizes the confidence sets.  
• Alternative feedback models. Since the lower bound hinges on pure bandit losses, it would be interesting to consider an intermediate feedback model (semi-bandit or episodic full feedback) to see how the regret scales down.  
• Reducing constants and logarithmic factors. The main theorem hides log (K/δ) and det(Mk,h) terms. A discussion of the leading constants could guide practical tuning of λ, η, and γ.

Recommendation  

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold  

This paper addresses a significant open problem in adversarial reinforcement learning with linear function approximation and unknown transitions. Its combination of occupancy‐measure methods, novel regression via imaginary next states, and tight lower bounds represents a substantial theoretical advance. While the dependence on |S|² and the computational claims could be clarified, the core contributions and rigorous analysis merit acceptance.

To this end, this paper proposes to use an imaginary next state to construct the regression target. Clarity: This paper is clear in general.Theorems have no ambiguity to me. Technical challenges and the corresponding solutions are described clearly. Related work is also discussed properly with now ambiguity. 


Quality: The quality is good. This is a purely theoretical paper and the theorems are solid. The proof feels correct to me, though I did not check all the details. 

Novelty: The paper is novel. The technical contributions, e.g. how to construct the confidence set of the transition parameter in the adversarial setting; how to choose the imaginary next state; are all good ideas in my opinion.


Reproducibility: This is purely theoretical work with no empirical result. The proof looks reproducible to me as it is written clearly and not very hard to read.

Summary Of The Review:
My current recommendation is accept. 

The reasons are the following:

1. Solid contribution: this paper gives the answer to a quite challenging and important field of reinforcement learning theory. 

2. Some novelty: to solve certain technical challenges, some novel ideas have been proposed.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies RL in linear mixture MDP with unknown transition, adversarial loss, and bandit feedback. This paper gives the first algorithm in this setting whose regret scales with sqrt(#episode) and supplements a lower bound, which matches the upper bound for the $d$ and $K$ dependency, where $K$ is #epsiode and $d$ is the ambient dimension of the linear mixture MDP.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
### Strength

1. This paper studies a reasonable setting of "adversarial" RL with linear function approximation, and designs and proves the first algorithm with sqrt(K) regret. 
2. This paper goes beyond the classical VTR (value-targeted regression) framework of analysis for linear mixture MDP, coming up with a novel technique (imaginary roll out of state) to cope the challenge arouse in adversarial setting.

### Weakness

1. There are many settings of linear RL theory besides linear mixture MDP, such as linear MDP. Also, recently, there are unified framework such as Bellman-rank and bilinear classes for RL with general function approximation. In particular, these frameworks include linear mixture MDP as their special cases. While the theory of RL with function approximation is certainly an important topic, it could become much less important when the scope is restricted to linear mixture MDP.
2. This paper does not prove tight lower bound to complete the theoretical research in their setting.
