reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper addresses a very practical and under-explored scenario in semi-supervised learning (SSL): when labels are missing not at random (MNAR). Under MNAR, the labeled and unlabeled data follow different class distributions, which leads to biased pseudo-label imputation and a collapse in performance of typical SSL methods. The authors propose Pseudo-Rectifying Guidance (PRG), which tracks class-level transitions of pseudo-labels over epochs, builds a class transition matrix, and then uses a Markov random walk on a dynamically constructed graph over this matrix to rescale pseudo-labels. By doing so, PRG softly “encourages” the model to revisit rare classes that would otherwise be overwhelmed by popular ones, thereby mitigating confirmation bias in the self-training loop. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, mini-ImageNet, and even tabular MNIST demonstrate that PRG yields substantial accuracy and geometric mean improvements over FixMatch, CADR, DARP, and other baselines in various MNAR protocols—including those proposed by CADR, our own fixed-nL protocol, and DARP’s mismatched distributions.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Pedagogical Quality.  The manuscript is exceptionally well written.  Section 1 carefully motivates why the conventional matched-distribution assumption fails in real labeling pipelines.  Figures 1 and 2 vividly illustrate the imbalance and the collapse of pseudo-rectifying ability in FixMatch under MNAR.  The narrative in Section 2 on related work places PRG in the context of distribution alignment and class-imbalanced SSL, making clear what existing approaches lack.

2. Intuitive, Simple Methodology.  PRG rests on a straightforward idea: if a model repeatedly oscillates between two classes in its pseudo-label assignments, those classes must be “similar” and deserving of extra pseudo-labeling attention.  This intuition leads naturally to the definition of the class tracking matrix C (Eq. 5) and its normalization into a transition matrix H.  The subsequent rescaling of the soft pseudo-labels (Eqs. 6–7) is both easy to implement (just a few matrix operations) and compatible with off-the-shelf SSL learners—demonstrated by plugging PRG into FixMatch and even UPS or VIME for tabular data.  The ablation study in Appendix B further confirms that the re-weighting scheme in Eq. 6 is critical to stabilize class transitions.

3. Strong Empirical Gains.  Under CADR’s protocol on CIFAR-10, PRG improves the accuracy of FixMatch from 51.10% to 94.04% at γ=50—a spectacular 42.94-point jump.  Similar lifts appear on CIFAR-100 and mini-ImageNet, and PRG beats CADR by as much as 15.11% in certain settings.  Figure 5 shows that PRG rapidly reduces class-wise pseudo-label error rates for both rare and popular classes, and Table 3 reports geometric mean (GM) improvements, highlighting PRG’s effectiveness on highly imbalanced tasks.  The additional results in Tables 9–10 confirm that PRG raises precision and recall especially on rare classes, and that PRG can handle both imbalanced labeled and imbalanced unlabeled distributions.

4. Reproducibility.  The authors provide detailed hyperparameter tables (Appendix C, Table 7), algorithm pseudocode (Appendix A), and an anonymous link to checkpoints and evaluation code.  They promise to release full source code upon acceptance.  The description of training schedules (220 epochs, WRN-28-2 backbone for CIFAR-10, etc.) matches standard practice, so practitioners should have no trouble reproducing these results.

Weaknesses  
1. Lack of Theoretical Justification.  While the intuition behind PRG is compelling, the paper does not offer a formal analysis of why the particular choice of H (and its row-normalized Markov walk formulation) is optimal or even well-behaved in all settings.  The reviewer wonders whether a monotone transformation of H or a likelihood-ratio derived weight matrix might work equally well or better.  Some basic theoretical insights—perhaps in a simplified linear regression or softmax-schedulable classification setting—would strengthen confidence in the approach and guide hyperparameter selection (e.g., the role of α).

2. Limited Novelty.  The core idea of aligning pseudo-label distributions to class priors or using dataset-level re-weighting has appeared in prior work (distribution alignment in Berthelot et al. (2020), DARP, Crest).  PRG extends this to a dynamic, class-level procedure, but one could argue that the methodological leap is incremental.  That said, the integration of class transitions from the model’s own history is an original twist, and its clear empirical superiority in MNAR is convincing.

3. Hyperparameter Sensitivity.  Although the authors report that α=1 and NB=128 work well across all experiments, it would be useful to know how sensitive PRG is to these choices beyond the brief grid in Figure 7.  Do smaller NB values lead to noisy C estimates?  How does performance vary if α is set much lower (e.g., 0.1) or higher (e.g., 5)?  Some additional curves or discussion in the main text would help practitioners.

4. Potential Interference with Negative Learning.  When the authors plugged PRG into UPS (Rizve et al., 2021), they observed only marginal gains—likely because UPS’s negative‐learning selection criterion can conflict with PRG’s rescaled soft labels.  A more detailed empirical or conceptual discussion of such interactions would be welcome, especially if one hopes to adapt PRG to other SSL frameworks.

Detailed Comments & Suggestions  
•  Figures 1 and 2: These are compelling visualizations of the MNAR problem.  Adding colorbars or numerical value bars to the heatmaps (class tracking matrix C in Fig. 4, confusion matrix in Fig. 2c) would make it easier for readers to gauge the magnitude of transitions.  

•  Table 8 (Appendix D.1) thoughtfully shows that naïve distribution alignment actually hurts under MNAR, underscoring PRG’s robustness to mismatched distributions.  This comparison is an excellent empirical demonstration of when DA breaks down.

•  The discussion in Section 3.1 on dataset-level vs. class-level guidance is illuminating.  It might help to include a brief running example—say, a 3-class toy problem—showing how C and H evolve over three epochs to produce a corrected pseudo-label for a sample of a rare class.

•  The pseudo-code in Appendix A is very clear.  It would be nice to mention in the main text that PRG can be seamlessly incorporated into existing SSL training loops with minimal overhead—indeed, per-iteration time hardly increases (as noted in Appendix C).

•  In Section 4.2, the conventional balanced SSL results (Table 4) show that PRG still matches or slightly outperforms FixMatch in standard settings.  This suggests PRG’s class transition guidance is not harmful even when MNAR does not occur.

•  Reproducibility: The authors should confirm that the anonymous code link includes scripts to generate the various MNAR splits (CADR’s, their own, DARP’s), since reproducing those protocols exactly is key.

Overall Recommendation  
This work offers a pragmatic, well-motivated, and empirically powerful solution to semi-supervised learning under label MNAR.  Despite its lack of formal theoretical analysis and a modest leap in methodological novelty, the clarity of exposition, strong performance gains, and ease of integration make it a valuable contribution.  Therefore, I recommend acceptance with a rating of 6: marginally above the threshold.  

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: A practical and challenging scenario called label Missing Not At Random (MNAR) is usually ignored in previous works on semi-supervised learning (SSL). In MNAR, the labeled and unlabeled data fall into different class distributions resulting in biased label imputation, which deteriorates the performance of SSL models. In this work, class transition tracking based Pseudo-Rectifying Guidance (PRG) is devised for MNAR. PRG unifies the history information of each class transition to activate the model’s enthusiasm for neglected classes, so as the quality of pseudo-labels on both popular classes and rare classes in MNAR could be improved.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
1. This paper studies an important and practical problem in semi-supervised learning, which has been not studied much.
2. This paper is written very well. The motivation of research is presented clearly along with the limitation of existing approaches.
3. It is good to represent an existing approach (Sohn et al., 2020) as the framework of PRG and explain why it fails at MNAR scenarios.

Weaknesses
1. The paper claims to solve the MNAR problem, but I think it solves only one part of the problem: when the distribution of labeled data is balanced. How well does PRG perform in the opposite setting, i.e., balanced labeled but imbalanced unlabeled data?
2. The intuitions for Equations (6) and (7) are insufficient, although they are the core techniques of the proposed approach. Why do we need to use H_{ij}’ instead of H_{ij}? Why do we need the L vector to reweight the transition matrix?
3. I have some concerns on the experiments. Please see the following: 
    1. I think the main competitor of PRG is CADR, which studies the same MNAR problem. However, the performance of CADR is presented only at Table 1 and missing from Table 2 and 3, and Figure 6. Why?
    2. In Table 1, the improvement of PRG from CADR is not always significant. In CIFAR-100 with \gamma=50, the accuracy of PRG is lower than that of CADR. When \gamma=200, the improvement is negligible considering the standard deviation. I don’t expect PRT to beat CDPR in all cases, but more explanations and analysis on the result would be helpful.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
A practical and challenging scenario called label Missing Not At Random (MNAR) is usually ignored in previous works on semi-supervised learning (SSL). In MNAR, the labeled and unlabeled data fall into different class distributions resulting in biased label imputation, which deteriorates the performance of SSL models. In this work, class transition tracking based Pseudo-Rectifying Guidance (PRG) is devised for MNAR. PRG unifies the history information of each class transition to activate the model’s enthusiasm for neglected classes, so as the quality of pseudo-labels on both popular classes and rare classes in MNAR could be improved.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths
1. This paper studies an important and practical problem in semi-supervised learning, which has been not studied much.
2. This paper is written very well. The motivation of research is presented clearly along with the limitation of existing approaches.
3. It is good to represent an existing approach (Sohn et al., 2020) as the framework of PRG and explain why it fails at MNAR scenarios.

Weaknesses
1. The paper claims to solve the MNAR problem, but I think it solves only one part of the problem: when the distribution of labeled data is balanced. How well does PRG perform in the opposite setting, i.e., balanced labeled but imbalanced unlabeled data?
2. The intuitions for Equations (6) and (7) are insufficient, although they are the core techniques of the proposed approach. Why do we need to use H_{ij}’ instead of H_{ij}? Why do we need the L vector to reweight the transition matrix?
3. I have some concerns on the experiments. Please see the following: 
    1. I think the main competitor of PRG is CADR, which studies the same MNAR problem. However, the performance of CADR is presented only at Table 1 and missing from Table 2 and 3, and Figure 6. Why?
    2. In Table 1, the improvement of PRG from CADR is not always significant. In CIFAR-100 with \gamma=50, the accuracy of PRG is lower than that of CADR. When \gamma=200, the improvement is negligible considering the standard deviation. I don’t expect PRT to beat CDPR in all cases, but more explanations and analysis on the result would be helpful.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
I think is paper is clearly written with a strong motivation, novel ideas, and sufficient reproducibility.

Summary Of The Review:
I liked reading this paper, and I think it proposes good ideas to an important problem. My main concerns are that (a) this paper solves only a part of the MNAR problem, and (b) the accuracy improvement is not as strong as the authors claim in the paper, and there are not sufficient analysis and explanation on the result, i.e., Table 1.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper tackles a practical and under‐explored scenario in semi‐supervised learning (SSL) called label Missing Not At Random (MNAR), where labeled and unlabeled data have mismatched class distributions. The authors propose a novel Pseudo‐Rectifying Guidance (PRG) framework that dynamically tracks class transitions during self‐training and uses a Markov random walk on a class‐transition graph to guide future pseudo‐label assignments. By monitoring how often samples flip from one class to another (encoded in the class tracking matrix C) and normalizing those transitions into a stochastic matrix H (then re‐weighted into H′ via batch‐level and class‐level statistics), PRG gently “nudges” the model to revisit neglected classes. Empirical results on CIFAR‐10, CIFAR‐100, and mini‐ImageNet, under several MNAR protocols, show that PRG consistently outperforms both conventional SSL methods (e.g., FixMatch) and recent MNAR‐specific approaches (e.g., CADR) by a sizable margin.

Strengths  
1. Important and Practical Problem.  This work addresses a realistic data‐collection issue in SSL—imbalanced labeling bias, which occurs naturally when some classes are harder to label or more prevalent in scraped datasets. By focusing on MNAR, the paper explores a setting closer to real‐world deployments than the balanced, matched distributions usually assumed.  
2. Clear Motivation and Framing.  The authors carefully motivate why conventional SSL methods degrade under MNAR, for example by showing in Figure 2 that FixMatch’s pseudo‐rectifying ability collapses when the labeled set overrepresents certain classes. The description of “pseudo‐rectifying” as the per‐epoch correction of wrong pseudo‐labels is intuitive and helps the reader understand the root cause—confirmation bias amplified by distribution mismatch.  
3. Methodological Novelty.  PRG introduces a class‐level guidance mechanism: a graph built on historical class transitions, modeled via a Markov random walk, to softly reweight pseudo‐label distributions (Equations 6–7). Unlike distribution‐alignment techniques that assume matched priors, PRG only relies on dynamically observed transition frequencies, making it well‐suited for MNAR. The idea of mining self‐correction attempts already present in the training trajectory is both elegant and conceptually new.  
4. Strong Empirical Gains.  Under CADR’s protocol (γ up to 100), the method yields up to +15.11% absolute gain over CADR on CIFAR‐10 and up to +23.37% over standard FixMatch. Results in Table 1, the geometric mean scores in Table 3, and class‐wise error/recall‐precision breakdowns in the Appendix all confirm PRG’s consistent advantage in correcting rare‐class predictions under severe imbalance.  
5. Reproducibility.  The paper is well‐written, with pseudocode in Appendix A, a full listing of hyper‐parameters in Table 7, and a promise to release code upon acceptance. The publicly available checkpoints and evaluation scripts further bolster reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
1. Partial Coverage of MNAR Scenarios.  The manuscript mainly evaluates the case where the labeled data are highly imbalanced and the unlabeled data remain balanced (as in CADR’s protocol). The opposite scenario—balanced labeled data but imbalanced unlabeled data—receives only a brief mention and limited results (e.g., Figure 6b, Appendix D.2). Because such cases can arise when large‐scale unlabeled corpora are themselves skewed, a more thorough empirical study or at least a concise discussion on PRG’s behavior in this reverse MNAR setting would strengthen the claim of generality.  
2. Insufficient Intuition for Core Equations.  Equations (6) and (7) lie at the heart of PRG, yet the paper offers only a brief “rescale at class‐ and batch‐level” description. In particular, the rationale for including the L‐vector (average class prediction counts) and the precise role of the normalization denominator Z are not fully unpacked. A supplemental paragraph explaining how these reweighting terms control the “intensity” of activated transitions—preventing both under‐ and over‐transitioning—would help readers grasp why H′ yields better guidance than the raw transition matrix H.  
3. Limited Analysis of Comparative Results.  
   a. CADR Comparison Across Tables and Figures.  Since CADR directly targets MNAR, it is arguably the main baseline. Yet CADR results appear in Table 1 but are missing from Tables 2 and 3 and from many subplots in Figure 6. A unified presentation that tracks CADR side by side with PRG under all protocols (our protocol, CADR’s, DARP’s, etc.) would clarify where PRG gains most and whether certain regimes (e.g., CIFAR‐100, γ=50) present exceptions.  
   b. Statistical Significance.  In a few settings (e.g., CIFAR‐100 with γ=50 in Table 1), PRG’s improvement over CADR is marginal or within one standard deviation. The paper would benefit from a short error‐bar analysis or paired‐test discussion explaining why PRG underperforms CADR in those corner cases—perhaps due to noisy transition estimates on very sparse classes—and whether tuning α or NB could mitigate that.  

Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
Overall, the paper is clearly structured, with a logical progression from problem statement to algorithm to experiments. The concept of tracking pseudo‐label flips to build a class transition graph is novel in the SSL literature. Pseudocode, hyper‐parameter tables, and publicly shared code/checkpoints ensure that the results can be reproduced by other practitioners.

Final Recommendation  
Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
I believe this paper makes a noteworthy contribution by introducing a fresh, graph‐based mechanism for guiding pseudo‐label correction in Semi‐Supervised Learning under MNAR. The method is well motivated, shows strong empirical gains, and is reproducible. Addressing the above weaknesses—especially by providing more intuition for Equations 6–7 and a fuller set of comparisons with CADR across all experimental tables—would further solidify the work. Nonetheless, given the importance of the MNAR problem and the promising performance of PRG, I recommend acceptance.

reviews.text:
Review for “Towards Semi-Supervised Learning with Non-Random Missing Labels”

Summary  
This paper addresses the practical and challenging setting of label Missing Not At Random (MNAR) in semi-supervised learning (SSL), where the class distributions of labeled and unlabeled data are mismatched, leading to biased pseudo-label imputation and degraded performance of conventional SSL methods. The authors propose Pseudo-Rectifying Guidance (PRG), which exploits the history of class transitions in the pseudo-rectifying process by constructing a class tracking matrix and modeling it via a Markov random walk over classes. The resulting transition probabilities are used to rescale soft pseudo-labels at each epoch, actively promoting transitions into under-represented classes and thereby mitigating confirmation bias. Experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, mini-ImageNet, and even tabular MNIST demonstrate that PRG substantially outperforms state-of-the-art SSL methods (e.g., FixMatch, CADR, DARP, Crest) under various MNAR protocols, with gains up to 15% in accuracy and large improvements in geometric mean scores.

Strengths  
1. Clear motivation for MNAR: The paper convincingly argues that in real annotation scenarios, ensuring a balanced labeled set is often infeasible, making MNAR almost inevitable. The introductory examples (Fig. 1 and Fig. 2) effectively illustrate how conventional methods like FixMatch suffer catastrophic error rates on rare classes.  
2. Novelty of focusing on label transitions: Leveraging the frequencies of pseudo-label class transitions recorded in a dynamic class tracking matrix to guide future rectifications is an original idea. Modeling these transitions as a Markov random walk (Eqs. 5–6) to derive class-level rectifying weights is particularly interesting.  
3. Extensive empirical validation: The authors evaluate PRG under three MNAR protocols (CADR’s, our fixed-nL, and DARP’s) across multiple datasets. Results in Tables 1–2 and Figure 5 show that PRG consistently lifts performance over strong baselines. The ablations on hyperparameters (α and NB in Figure 7) and re-weighting schemes (Table 5) bolster confidence in the approach’s robustness.  
4. Broad applicability: The paper even demonstrates PRG’s effectiveness beyond vision, by integrating it into VIME on tabular MNIST (Table 11), suggesting the class-transition guidance mechanism is generally useful.

Weaknesses  
1. Hard to follow Section 3.1: The presentation of Pseudo-Rectifying Guidance in Section 3.1 is dense. For example, the symbol ξ (used to denote the scaling weight vector η in Eq. 3) is never formally introduced or defined, and it is absent from the pseudocode (Algorithms 1–2). A clear definition of all symbols and a step-by-step example of how ˜p is computed from p and H′ would greatly aid understanding.  
2. Confusing Figure 3: Although visually appealing, Figure 3 packs too much information without a detailed legend or numbered steps. The black box drawn around entry (1,1) of the class tracking matrix is never explained in the caption or text and only serves to distract. Replacing it with a callout that visually traces one sample’s pseudo-rectifying flow through the graph, transition matrix, and final label normalization would clarify the mechanism.

Clarity, Reproducibility, and Writing  
The overall writing is solid but could benefit from additional exposition in the methodological core. In particular, a concise summary at the end of Section 3.1, reiterating how Â pe+1 is combined with model outputs, and a brief run-through of Algorithm 1 with concrete dimensions (e.g., k=10, NB=128, BU=448) would bridge the gap between theory and implementation. On reproducibility, the authors provide an anonymous code link, detailed hyperparameters in Appendix C (Table 7), and algorithmic pseudocode, which should allow the community to verify and build upon this work.

Detailed Suggestions  
• In Section 3.1, explicitly define η (or ξ) and map it to the rectifying weight matrix A. A short paragraph showing the numeric effect on a toy 3-class example would remove ambiguity.  
• In Figure 3, remove the unexplained black box on (1,1). Instead, annotate each block with step numbers (1–4) and reference these in the text: (1) class transition tracking, (2) transition matrix normalization, (3) batch/class rescaling, (4) pseudo-rectifying guidance.  
• Clarify how α (class invariance coefficient) is chosen and its impact on the diagonal entries Hii. Although Appendix B gives theoretical intuition, a one-sentence reminder in the main text would help readers.  
• Ensure consistency between notation in the main body and pseudocode: if gθ, Normalize, and A are used in Eq. (3), mirror these names and symbols in Algorithm 1.  

Summary and Recommendation  
Overall, this paper tackles a practically significant extension of SSL, namely MNAR, with a novel, well-motivated method that demonstrates strong empirical gains. While some parts of the exposition—especially Section 3.1 and Figure 3—remain hard to digest at first reading, these are addressable through improved definitions and figure legends. Given the strength of the technical contribution and reproducible performance improvements, I recommend acceptance. Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold).

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper tackles semi-supervised learning with a focus on the most challenging setting of when there is a mismatch between the distributions of labelled and unlabelled data. The proposed method, PRG, uses the history of class transitions to sway the pseudo-labelling mechanism towards under-represented classes. This works by exploiting pairwise similarities between classes: classes that are often confused must be similar (with respect to the predictions of the model) and thus, if the model predicts one of them, we should also consider the others as possible pseudo-labels with certain probability. The authors validate PRG on CIFAR and mini-Imagenet with promising results, outperforming previous similar methods.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
### Strength
- The paper proposes a simple and seemingly effective method that improves previous approaches for semi-supervised learning with imbalanced and mismatched class distributions. That is a relevant contribution that, I believe, would be useful both in practical applications and future research.
- Empirically the proposed method works well, outperforming previous competing methods by significant margins.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation of the proposed methods is somewhat weak. 
    - The definition of the transition matrix $\bf H$ is not very well motivated. It is not entire clear to me why Equation (6) is preferable to simply estimating $\bf H$ from the transitions observed in a batch of data. At least that would be a simple and telling baseline to compare against that, I believe, would add to the paper.
    - The authors say "the history of class transitions can point the way for bias removal on label imputation with an abnormal propensity on different classes caused by mismatched distributions in MNAR" but only hint as to why that is the case. We are told PRG works because it encourages the model to predict under-represented classes, but that does not necessarily require a history of transitions nor does it attenuate "over-learning the samples of the labeled popular classes". Empirically, PRG seems to work well, supporting the authors' claim to some extent, but in the current version of the paper the connection between the history of transitions and mismatched class distributions is rather loose. To be clear, I am not suggesting such a connection does not exist. I am just stating that it is not obvious (and thus potentially interesting) and the paper in its current version does not explain it very clearly.
    - The random walk motivation is interesting but feels under-explored. Why take a simple step in the random walk? If we took $k$ steps, i.e., multiply by $\bf H^k$ instead of $\bf H$ in Equation (7), we could uncover more complex patterns of misclassification than simple pairwise class relations.  For instance, if classes A and B are often misclassified as class C, but the model rarely classifies A as B and vice-versa, PRG would not capture this sort of relationship even though the transition matrix does.

- Given that the motivation of the paper is not very grounded, the experiments seem not thorough enough since they are limited to CIFAR and mini-imagenet. CIFAR is notably noisy, which my benefit PRG. It would be interesting to see if the history of transitions is a useful artefact for general types of data other than images. Even simple and easy to run tabular datasets would already contribute to the overall message of the paper.

- The paper is harder to follow than one would expect for such a simple and intuitive approach. Parts of the text are confusing and some of the notation is unnecessary or not properly introduced. I comment on some examples of this in the questions and minor issues below, and in the clarity section.

### Questions
- What exactly is $\xi$ computing? "Rectifying weight vector" is not a very precise description.
- In Equation (6), isn't $\sum_{d=1}^k L_d$ always equal to 1? So the first term on right-hand side of (6) is just the ratio of total number of predictions over the number of predictions that changed class (in a given batch), and is thus always larger than one?
- If $\bf H$ is a proper transition matrix, while do we need to renormalise $p$? Is it because of the $\xi$ term?
- In Figure 7, it is interesting that the performance drops for larger batch sizes. One would expect the estimate of the transition matrix to be improve, which in turn should translate into better or at least similar performance. Do the authors have an idea as why that is the case? Could it be that the learning rate, if kept fixed in the ablation study, was too large for a batch size of 512?

### Minor issues
- The authors use $\max(p)$ as a measure of the model's confidence. That is only one of the possible ways to quantify confidence and that choice should be mentioned more explicitly. Also, the authors imply that the model is more confident ($\max(p)$ is higher) when classifying classes that appear frequently. While this might be intuitive, I think this is an empirical observation that does not always hold for every data point and for arbitrary models, and in particular not for neural networks. If this is indeed an empirical observation, it should be made clear.
- In Figure 4, I cannot see the pattern the authors describe in "the lower right corner (corresponding to the labeled popular classes) of the heatmap is getting lighter and always lighter than the upper left corner." Maybe the Figure is not showing right or the matrices need to be renormalised to show the desired effect?
- Probably best to mention $C_{ii}=0$ already in Equation (5).
- The missing label indicator $m$ is only used once. Not sure it is worth introducing it.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
To the best of my knowledge the ideas in the paper are novel, and I have no reason to believe the results are not reproducible. The presentation of the paper leaves something to be desired. The overall motivation is not exposed clearly, as discussed previously, and the text has a number of typos and some rather unclear parts. For instance, I failed to understand the sentences below.
- "The propensity of rectifying the labels belonging to the same class to different classes is different".
- "PRG recalls those “old face” classes once assigned to the unlabeled data".
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The main idea of the paper is interesting and seems to work well in a relevant scenario. The methods are rather simple, but I see that as a potential strength, facilitating their application in future work. However, the exposition of the paper is not clear and the main ideas and motivation are under-explored or not conveyed plainly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

- In Figure 7, it is interesting that the performance drops for larger batch sizes. One would expect the estimate of the transition matrix to be improve, which in turn should translate into better or at least similar performance. Do the authors have an idea as why that is the case? Could it be that the learning rate, if kept fixed in the ablation study, was too large for a batch size of 512?

### Minor issues
- The authors use $\max(p)$ as a measure of the model's confidence. That is only one of the possible ways to quantify confidence and that choice should be mentioned more explicitly. Also, the authors imply that the model is more confident ($\max(p)$ is higher) when classifying classes that appear frequently. While this might be intuitive, I think this is an empirical observation that does not always hold for every data point and for arbitrary models, and in particular not for neural networks. If this is indeed an empirical observation, it should be made clear.
- In Figure 4, I cannot see the pattern the authors describe in "the lower right corner (corresponding to the labeled popular classes) of the heatmap is getting lighter and always lighter than the upper left corner." Maybe the Figure is not showing right or the matrices need to be renormalised to show the desired effect?
- Probably best to mention $C_{ii}=0$ already in Equation (5).
- The missing label indicator $m$ is only used once. Not sure it is worth introducing it.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
To the best of my knowledge the ideas in the paper are novel, and I have no reason to believe the results are not reproducible. The presentation of the paper leaves something to be desired. The overall motivation is not exposed clearly, as discussed previously, and the text has a number of typos and some rather unclear parts. For instance, I failed to understand the sentences below.
- "The propensity of rectifying the labels belonging to the same class to different classes is different".
- "PRG recalls those “old face” classes once assigned to the unlabeled data".

Summary Of The Review:
The main idea of the paper is interesting and seems to work well in a relevant scenario. The methods are rather simple, but I see that as a potential strength, facilitating their application in future work. However, the exposition of the paper is not clear and the main ideas and motivation are under-explored or not conveyed plainly.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
The paper tackles semi-supervised learning (SSL) under the challenging scenario where the class distributions of labeled and unlabeled data do not match, known as label Missing Not At Random (MNAR). The proposed method, Pseudo-Rectifying Guidance (PRG), tracks the history of class transitions during pseudo-labeling and constructs a class tracking matrix \(C\). By treating these transitions as edges in a graph and modeling them with a Markov random walk, PRG obtains a transition matrix \(H\) (Eq. (5)–(6)) that captures the propensity for a sample’s predicted label to flip from one class to another. During training, PRG rescales the standard pseudo-labels via \( \tilde p = \mathrm{Normalize}(H'_{\hat p}\odot g_\theta(p)) \) (Eq. (7)), gently encouraging occasional transitions toward under-represented (“rare”) classes. The authors evaluate PRG by plugging it into FixMatch and demonstrate substantial accuracy gains across various MNAR protocols on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and mini-ImageNet, often exceeding previous methods by more than 10–15%.

Strengths:  
- The method is conceptually simple, requiring no additional network components, and can be plugged into any self-training SSL framework. This low architectural overhead is an appealing quality for practical adoption.  
- Empirically, PRG yields consistent and significant improvements over strong baselines—including FixMatch, DARP, Crest, and even the recent MNAR-focused CADR—under multiple imbalanced and mismatched class distribution protocols (Tabs. 1–3, Figs. 5–6).  
- The authors provide a fairly extensive empirical study, including ablations on the class invariance coefficient \(\alpha\) and batch count \(N_B\) (Fig. 7), and report additional metrics such as geometric mean (GM) that are appropriate for imbalanced scenarios (Tab. 3).  
- The paper includes pseudocode (Alg. 1–2), hyperparameter tables (Tab. 7), and a link to anonymized code and checkpoints, supporting reproducibility.

Weaknesses:

1. Motivation of the Transition Matrix \(H\) is Under-Explored.  
   - While Eq. (5) defines \(C_{ij}\) as the averaged frequency of class transitions from \(i\to j\), it is not entirely clear why the particular re-weighting in Eq. (6)—which normalizes \(H\) row-wise and then rescales by both a class-level factor \(\sum_d C_{i d}\) and a batch-level factor \(\sum_d L_d\)—is preferable to a simpler baseline that directly uses the empirical transition frequencies. A direct comparison to such a baseline (e.g., \(H\) estimated per mini-batch or a uniform re-scaling) would help to isolate whether the two-level normalization is essential.  
   - The authors argue that “the history of class transitions can point the way for bias removal” when imputation is skewed by popular classes (Sec. 1). However, the mechanistic link between these recorded transitions and the alleviation of confirmation bias in MNAR remains a bit loose. For instance, it would be helpful to see a toy example or a more formal argument explaining how an under-represented class’s past transitions (which become rare over training as in Fig. 4) revive its predicted probability mass rather than merely acting as noise.

2. Random Walk Step Depth Not Fully Explored.  
   - The paper motivates modeling class transitions as a Markov random walk on the class graph (Sec. 3.2) and applies one-step transitions \(H\). A natural extension—taking \(k>1\) steps via multiplying by \(H^k\)—could capture higher-order relationships (e.g., class A often transitions to B via C). Although the authors briefly mention in Sec. B that multi-step transitions were tried and performance declined (Tab. 6), a more detailed discussion or empirical curve showing the trade-off would be valuable, especially since different datasets might benefit from deeper propagation.  
   - The specific rationale for choosing one-step transitions as the design point remains rooted in “simplicity” (App. B), but a clearer articulation of when longer walks would or would not help (e.g., as graph diameter grows) would strengthen the conceptual contribution.

3. Limited Data Modalities.  
   - All experiments focus on vision benchmarks (CIFAR, ImageNet subset). CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 in particular are known to exhibit high levels of label noise and artifacts (e.g., ambiguous low-resolution images). It is possible that the class transition signal exploited by PRG is stronger in these noisy scenarios, which may inflate results. Simple tabular or text datasets (e.g., UCI benchmarks, sentiment classification with partial labels) could test whether the transition-based guidance generalizes beyond vision.  
   - At present, the lack of non-image experiments leaves open the question of PRG’s broader applicability.

4. Paper Clarity and Notation Issues.  
   - Several symbols and terms are introduced without precise definitions. For instance, the rectifying weight vector \(\xi\) (Sec. 3.1) is mentioned but not formally equated to the rows of \(A\) or \(H\). Clarifying that \(\xi=A_{\hat p}\) or \(\xi=H'_{\hat p}\) would help.  
   - Equation (6) employs both \(\sum_{d}L_d\) and \(\sum_{d,d'}C_{dd'}\). The text suggests \(\sum L_d=1\), but in practice it equals the total number of predictions per batch; this should be stated explicitly.  
   - The use of \(\max(p)\) as the model’s “confidence” (Sec. 1) is only one possible metric. While widely used in SSL, it should be called out as a design choice, and any empirical evidence that \(\max(p)\) indeed correlates with class frequency in MNAR would support the assumption.  
   - Some sentences are difficult to parse, e.g., “PRG recalls those ‘old face’ classes once assigned to the unlabeled data,” and “the propensity of rectifying the labels belonging to the same class to different classes is different.” A thorough proofreading to tighten language and correct minor typos would improve readability significantly.

Additional Questions and Suggestions:  
- Could the authors include a baseline that computes \(H\) directly from the current mini-batch (without moving averages) to test the importance of smoothing over \(N_B\) batches?  
- In the ablation on batch count \(N_B\) (Fig. 7 right), performance drops for very small and very large \(N_B\). Could this be due to the staleness of transition estimates at large \(N_B\)? A plot of transition matrix drift over iterations might shed light.  
- The pseudo-code in Alg. 1–2 is helpful, but it treats the class-level guidance as a black box. Annotating each step with references to the equations (e.g., line 6 uses Eq. (6)) would make it easier to follow.  
- To further illustrate the bias removal effect, class-wise learning curves for the rare classes (e.g., recall over epochs) could show how PRG rescues their accuracy compared to the rapid collapse in FixMatch (Fig. 2b vs. 5a).  
- If possible, adding one or two small non-vision datasets—even something like a binary classification with two rare classes in a tabular UCI dataset—would demonstrate PRG’s generality.

Summary of the Review:  
PRG addresses an important and practically relevant extension of SSL—handling MNAR label bias—via a lightweight graph-based pseudo-label correction. The method is simple, broadly applicable, and yields strong empirical gains. However, the paper’s core motivation for the specific transition re-weighting, deeper random walk justification, and reliance on vision benchmarks are areas that would benefit from further clarification or additional experiments. The presentation also requires polishing in notation and writing. With these enhancements, PRG would be a more compelling and broadly convincing contribution.

Rating:  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper tackles semi-supervised learning with a focus on the most challenging setting of when there is a mismatch between the distributions of labelled and unlabelled data. The proposed method, PRG, uses the history of class transitions to sway the pseudo-labelling mechanism towards under-represented classes. This works by exploiting pairwise similarities between classes: classes that are often confused must be similar (with respect to the predictions of the model) and thus, if the model predicts one of them, we should also consider the others as possible pseudo-labels with certain probability. The authors validate PRG on CIFAR and mini-Imagenet with promising results, outperforming previous similar methods.

Strength And Weaknesses:
### Strength
- The paper proposes a simple and seemingly effective method that improves previous approaches for semi-supervised learning with imbalanced and mismatched class distributions. That is a relevant contribution that, I believe, would be useful both in practical applications and future research.
- Empirically the proposed method works well, outperforming previous competing methods by significant margins.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation of the proposed methods is somewhat weak. 
    - The definition of the transition matrix $\bf H$ is not very well motivated. It is not entire clear to me why Equation (6) is preferable to simply estimating $\bf H$ from the transitions observed in a batch of data. At least that would be a simple and telling baseline to compare against that, I believe, would add to the paper.
    - The authors say "the history of class transitions can point the way for bias removal on label imputation with an abnormal propensity on different classes caused by mismatched distributions in MNAR" but only hint as to why that is the case. We are told PRG works because it encourages the model to predict under-represented classes, but that does not necessarily require a history of transitions nor does it attenuate "over-learning the samples of the labeled popular classes". Empirically, PRG seems to work well, supporting the authors' claim to some extent, but in the current version of the paper the connection between the history of transitions and mismatched class distributions is rather loose. To be clear, I am not suggesting such a connection does not exist. I am just stating that it is not obvious (and thus potentially interesting) and the paper in its current version does not explain it very clearly.
    - The random walk motivation is interesting but feels under-explored. Why take a simple step in the random walk? If we took $k$ steps, i.e., multiply by $\bf H^k$ instead of $\bf H$ in Equation (7), we could uncover more complex patterns of misclassification than simple pairwise class relations.  For instance, if classes A and B are often misclassified as class C, but the model rarely classifies A as B and vice-versa, PRG would not capture this sort of relationship even though the transition matrix does.

- Given that the motivation of the paper is not very grounded, the experiments seem not thorough enough since they are limited to CIFAR and mini-imagenet. CIFAR is notably noisy, which my benefit PRG. It would be interesting to see if the history of transitions is a useful artefact for general types of data other than images. Even simple and easy to run tabular datasets would already contribute to the overall message of the paper.

- The paper is harder to follow than one would expect for such a simple and intuitive approach. Parts of the text are confusing and some of the notation is unnecessary or not properly introduced. I comment on some examples of this in the questions and minor issues below, and in the clarity section.

### Questions
- What exactly is $\xi$ computing? "Rectifying weight vector" is not a very precise description.
- In Equation (6), isn't $\sum_{d=1}^k L_d$ always equal to 1? So the first term on right-hand side of (6) is just the ratio of total number of predictions over the number of predictions that changed class (in a given batch), and is thus always larger than one?
- If $\bf H$ is a proper transition matrix, while do we need to renormalise $p$? Is it because of the $\xi$ term?
- In Figure 7, it is interesting that the performance drops for larger batch sizes. One would expect the estimate of the transition matrix to be improve, which in turn should translate into better or at least similar performance. Do the authors have an idea as why that is the case? Could it be that the learning rate, if kept fixed in the ablation study, was too large for a batch size of 512?

### Minor issues
- The authors use $\max(p)$ as a measure of the model's confidence. That is only one of the possible ways to quantify confidence and that choice should be mentioned more explicitly. Also, the authors imply that the model is more confident ($\max(p)$ is higher) when classifying classes that appear frequently. While this might be intuitive, I think this is an empirical observation that does not always hold for every data point and for arbitrary models, and in particular not for neural networks. If this is indeed an empirical observation, it should be made clear.
- In Figure 4, I cannot see the pattern the authors describe in "the lower right corner (corresponding to the labeled popular classes) of the heatmap is getting lighter and always lighter than the upper left corner." Maybe the Figure is not showing right or the matrices need to be renormalised to show the desired effect?
- Probably best to mention $C_{ii}=0$ already in Equation (5).
- The missing label indicator $m$ is only used once. Not sure it is worth introducing it.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
To the best of my knowledge the ideas in the paper are novel, and I have no reason to believe the results are not reproducible. The presentation of the paper leaves something to be desired. The overall motivation is not exposed clearly, as discussed previously, and the text has a number of typos and some rather unclear parts. For instance, I failed to understand the sentences below.
- "The propensity of rectifying the labels belonging to the same class to different classes is different".
- "PRG recalls those “old face” classes once assigned to the unlabeled data".

Summary Of The Review:
The main idea of the paper is interesting and seems to work well in a relevant scenario. The methods are rather simple, but I see that as a potential strength, facilitating their application in future work. However, the exposition of the paper is not clear and the main ideas and motivation are under-explored or not conveyed plainly.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Single-Stage Open-world Instance Segmentation with Cross-task Consistency Regularization

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a method for single-stage open-world instance segmentation. It is the first to uses the SOTA Mask2Former architecture for this task. Additionally, to tackle the problem of "missing annotations" while training for open-world segmentations with the limited category labels in the training dataset, the authors propose a foreground prediction branch along with a cross-task consistency loss. The authors further show that since the latter loss does not require any labels, it can also be effectively employed  to train networks in a semi-supervised manner. The authors show quantitatively the superior accuracy of their approach in comparison to the existing state-of-the-art approaches on Open-World instance segmentation, on several baseline datasets. Additionally, they also show the contribution of each component of their design via ablation studies and the usefulness of their proposed approach in semi-supervised learning.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
Novelty: This paper tackles the problem of open-world instance segmentation, which is a largely open, albeit an important problem in computer vision research. If solved it has huge implications in being able to go from successfully segmenting a few hundred categories of objects today to potentially infinite ones in the future. Hence, it is important to further develop this line of research. 

In terms of the proposed method itself, there are several novel aspects to it. The authors are the first to apply the SOTA transformer-based Mask2former architecture to the task of open-world instance segmentation. Given the known success of the Mask2former in the closed-world setting it is not surprising that also helps to significantly boost accuracies for the proposed method in the open-world setting. Hence, I consider this as a somewhat incremental contribution. Nevertheless, it is to the authors' credit that they are the first to numerically show this to be true. 

The more interesting aspects of the authors' method, however, are the proposed foreground prediction branch and the self-supervised cross-task loss associated with it, which are designed to deal with the problem of "missing annotations" in the COCO dataset. I found this design to be clever, task-specific, conceptually correct and numerically shown to help in boosting the accuracy for open-world instance segmentation.

Significance of results:
In absolute terms the proposed method helps to significantly improve the accuracy of the open-world instance segmentation task. That said, the major boost comes from using Mask2Former and less from the other components. Nevertheless, this work is significant in advancing accuracy of this task. Additionally the proposed background prediction module is shown to be multi-purpose. It can be used both for open-world segmentation and for semi-supervised training.

Weaknesses
Claims:
1. The authors claim in the introduction "We propose a Singe-stage Open-world Instance Segmentation (SOIS) for the first time". This is technically not true. The previous GGN work is also a single-stage method based on SOLOv2. In relation to the existing work, the novel contribution of this work is to be the first to apply a transformer-based Mask2Former architecture to the task of open-world instance segmentation. The authors should correct their claims appropriately.

Experiments:
2. How sensitive is the proposed method's success to choosing the appropriate weight for the $L_c$ loss term to negate the effect of the network incorrectly penalizing missing detections? Does it need to be set differently for the different datasets?

3. In Table 1, what is the intuition behind the proposed foreground prediction loss helping in the intra-dataset setting? If it is mostly designed to catch un-annotated detections, how does it help to segment the labeled annotations?

4. Section 4.5. 
It would be better to call the Fully-SOIS to "Labelled-Only-SOIS". The term "full" seems to imply that the full training set of OVU was used versus just the labeled 30%/50% set. Related to this point, in figure 5, for completeness the authors should also show the results of training with the full OVU training set, ie., the numbers from Table 6.

Minor points:
1. Introduction, Para 2: "which detects" --> "which detect"
2. In Fig.2, the masks don't correspond to the input image at all. Although they are meant to show the concept, nevertheless it would be better to at least show masks that are somewhat representative of the input scene and not those of faces!
3. In several places, "leaning" --> "learning"
4. Table 2, clarify what $s$, $m$ and $l$ mean.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: Mostly clear, but could be improved further by addressing the points noted in the weaknesses section above.

Quality: Meets the quality standards of an ICLR submission in terms of the technical contribution and presentation.

Novelty: Sufficiently novel in both the proposed method and the fairly open problem addressed.

Reproducibility: The method is well described. The supplementary provides the implementation details. The authors provide the code and have promised to release it.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall the paper makes a significant contribution in advancing the field of open-world instance segmentation. Hence I am leaning towards accepting this paper, with the caveat that the authors improve the claims and clarity of their paper as noted in the "weaknesses" section above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a method for single-stage open-world instance segmentation. It is the first to uses the SOTA Mask2Former architecture for this task. Additionally, to tackle the problem of "missing annotations" while training for open-world segmentations with the limited category labels in the training dataset, the authors propose a foreground prediction branch along with a cross-task consistency loss. The authors further show that since the latter loss does not require any labels, it can also be effectively employed  to train networks in a semi-supervised manner. The authors show quantitatively the superior accuracy of their approach in comparison to the existing state-of-the-art approaches on Open-World instance segmentation, on several baseline datasets. Additionally, they also show the contribution of each component of their design via ablation studies and the usefulness of their proposed approach in semi-supervised learning.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

If solved it has huge implications in being able to go from successfully segmenting a few hundred categories of objects today to potentially infinite ones in the future. Hence, it is important to further develop this line of research. 

In terms of the proposed method itself, there are several novel aspects to it. The authors are the first to apply the SOTA transformer-based Mask2former architecture to the task of open-world instance segmentation. Given the known success of the Mask2former in the closed-world setting it is not surprising that also helps to significantly boost accuracies for the proposed method in the open-world setting. Hence, I consider this as a somewhat incremental contribution. Nevertheless, it is to the authors' credit that they are the first to numerically show this to be true. 

The more interesting aspects of the authors' method, however, are the proposed foreground prediction branch and the self-supervised cross-task loss associated with it, which are designed to deal with the problem of "missing annotations" in the COCO dataset. I found this design to be clever, task-specific, conceptually correct and numerically shown to help in boosting the accuracy for open-world instance segmentation.

Significance of results:
In absolute terms the proposed method helps to significantly improve the accuracy of the open-world instance segmentation task. That said, the major boost comes from using Mask2Former and less from the other components. Nevertheless, this work is significant in advancing accuracy of this task. Additionally the proposed background prediction module is shown to be multi-purpose. It can be used both for open-world segmentation and for semi-supervised training.

Weaknesses

1. The authors claim in the introduction "We propose a Singe-stage Open-world Instance Segmentation (SOIS) for the first time". This is technically not true. The previous GGN work is also a single-stage method based on SOLOv2. In relation to the existing work, the novel contribution of this work is to be the first to apply a transformer-based Mask2Former architecture to the task of open-world instance segmentation. The authors should correct their claims appropriately.

Experiments:
2. How sensitive is the proposed method's success to choosing the appropriate weight for the $L_c$ loss term to negate the effect of the network incorrectly penalizing missing detections? Does it need to be set differently for the different datasets?

3. In Table 1, what is the intuition behind the proposed foreground prediction loss helping in the intra-dataset setting? If it is mostly designed to catch un-annotated detections, how does it help to segment the labeled annotations?

4. Section 4.5. 
It would be better to call the Fully-SOIS to "Labelled-Only-SOIS". The term "full" seems to imply that the full training set of OVU was used versus just the labeled 30%/50% set. Related to this point, in figure 5, for completeness the authors should also show the results of training with the full OVU training set, ie., the numbers from Table 6.

Minor points:
1. Introduction, Para 2: "which detects" --> "which detect"
2. In Fig.2, the masks don't correspond to the input image at all. Although they are meant to show the concept, nevertheless it would be better to at least show masks that are somewhat representative of the input scene and not those of faces!
3. In several places, "leaning" --> "learning"
4. Table 2, clarify what $s$, $m$ and $l$ mean.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: Mostly clear, but could be improved further by addressing the points noted in the weaknesses section above.

Quality: Meets the quality standards of an ICLR submission in terms of the technical contribution and presentation.

Novelty: Sufficiently novel in both the proposed method and the fairly open problem addressed.

Reproducibility: The method is well described. The supplementary provides the implementation details. The authors provide the code and have promised to release it.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall the paper makes a significant contribution in advancing the field of open-world instance segmentation. Hence I am leaning towards accepting this paper, with the caveat that the authors improve the claims and clarity of their paper as noted in the "weaknesses" section above.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper tackles the task of open-world instance segmentation in images (no predefined set of classes). The proposed method extends on an existing state-of-the-art single-stage (without employing a dedicated proposals procedure) approach, such as Mask2former, through the addition of a foreground segmentation branch. The foreground prediction map is used as an auxiliary supervisory signal for the instance prediction branch to increase the consistency among these maps. The consistency loss applied on top of these predictions also alleviates the problem of incomplete instance annotation which could be considered a core contribution of this work (working with labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously - semi-supervised scenario).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
* The tackled problem is challenging and of interest to the research community - open-world instance segmentation is far from being solved - since it implies the discovery of novel object instances that were not seen during training

Weaknesses:
* Problem statement description could be improved. The introductory section does not properly address the tackled task or the way in which this work contributes to current issues. 
* Hard to grasp from the paper what are the authors' contributions - I did not find the paper an easy read.
* The authors rename the Mask2former architecture as SOIS and wrongfully consider it their contribution since the architecture itself did not change as the authors stated themselves but rather the scenario in which it was used (extension to a semi-supervised scenario by employing a proxy constraint, in the form of a consistency loss between predicted masks of two existing branches in the architecture)

Other comments:
* Benchmarks are not referenced when first mentioned - same goes for COCO and UVO in the abstract - at least mention that these are datasets. 
* Figure 1 is crowded and hard to follow all of its parts. It would make more sense to split it into two parts - a, b, c - Part 1 and d, e, f, g - Part 2. In (b) consistency is misspelled and Cityscapes is misspelled in the figure caption.
* Figure 1c, 1d, 1e and 1f are not referenced in the paper.
* Figure 2 is not informative at all - What is the purpose of that backbone? - What is the format of the output? What is the input of the FCN in the foreground prediction branch and why not use the RGB image? The image caption does not complement the figure - most of the notations are not explained and the flow of the different branches is very hard to follow. Also, sentences such as "the foreground prediction branch segments a foreground region" can be derived from the naming of these components. Authors should highlight only the information that cannot be inferred solely from the naming.

Quality - The paper could be improved in terms of language and structure - there are also a number of typos and missing references that should be addressed. Most parts of Figure 1 are not referenced in the paper.

Novelty - I do not consider the contribution novel, but an extension of an existing state-of-the-art that was applied in a different learning setup that deals with missing annotations - not the fully-supervised case. For this purpose alone the authors apply a consistency loss computed as a segmentation loss between the instance segmentation branch and a foreground prediction branch and use this as a constraint in learning when labels are missing. 

Reproducibility - The authors have released the code in their supplementary material - the experiments should be easy to reproduce.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper tackles the task of open-world instance segmentation in images (no predefined set of classes). The proposed method extends on an existing state-of-the-art single-stage (without employing a dedicated proposals procedure) approach, such as Mask2former, through the addition of a foreground segmentation branch. The foreground prediction map is used as an auxiliary supervisory signal for the instance prediction branch to increase the consistency among these maps. The consistency loss applied on top of these predictions also alleviates the problem of incomplete instance annotation which could be considered a core contribution of this work (working with labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously - semi-supervised scenario).

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
* The tackled problem is challenging and of interest to the research community - open-world instance segmentation is far from being solved - since it implies the discovery of novel object instances that were not seen during training

Weaknesses:
* Problem statement description could be improved. The introductory section does not properly address the tackled task or the way in which this work contributes to current issues. 
* Hard to grasp from the paper what are the authors' contributions - I did not find the paper an easy read.
* The authors rename the Mask2former architecture as SOIS and wrongfully consider it their contribution since the architecture itself did not change as the authors stated themselves but rather the scenario in which it was used (extension to a semi-supervised scenario by employing a proxy constraint, in the form of a consistency loss between predicted masks of two existing branches in the architecture)

Other comments:
* Benchmarks are not referenced when first mentioned - same goes for COCO and UVO in the abstract - at least mention that these are datasets. 
* Figure 1 is crowded and hard to follow all of its parts. It would make more sense to split it into two parts - a, b, c - Part 1 and d, e, f, g - Part 2. In (b) consistency is misspelled and Cityscapes is misspelled in the figure caption.
* Figure 1c, 1d, 1e and 1f are not referenced in the paper.
* Figure 2 is not informative at all - What is the purpose of that backbone? - What is the format of the output? What is the input of the FCN in the foreground prediction branch and why not use the RGB image? The image caption does not complement the figure - most of the notations are not explained and the flow of the different branches is very hard to follow. Also, sentences such as "the foreground prediction branch segments a foreground region" can be derived from the naming of these components. Authors should highlight only the information that cannot be inferred solely from the naming.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity - Could be improved. The figure and caption do not complement each other. Also, there are notations in the paper that are not clearly defined (Section 3.2) What is Ki? The number of annotated object instances in a given image i? Should be explicitly specified.

Quality - The paper could be improved in terms of language and structure - there are also a number of typos and missing references that should be addressed. Most parts of Figure 1 are not referenced in the paper.

Novelty - I do not consider the contribution novel, but an extension of an existing state-of-the-art that was applied in a different learning setup that deals with missing annotations - not the fully-supervised case. For this purpose alone the authors apply a consistency loss computed as a segmentation loss between the instance segmentation branch and a foreground prediction branch and use this as a constraint in learning when labels are missing. 

Reproducibility - The authors have released the code in their supplementary material - the experiments should be easy to reproduce.

Summary Of The Review:
Besides the aforementioned issues with the current submission, I do not find it fit for publication not just for the structure or lack of clarity, but mostly due to its incremental contribution to existing works.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper addresses Open-World Instance Segmentation (OWIS), where models must discover and segment object instances without a fixed set of categories. Building on the recent Mask2Former framework, the authors propose what they call TOIS, which integrates a lightweight foreground prediction branch and enforces a cross-task consistency loss between the foreground map and per-instance masks. The key claimed benefit is that this consistency loss mitigates the effect of incomplete annotations in open-world datasets and even enables a semi-supervised variant that leverages unlabeled images. Extensive experiments on COCO→UVO, Cityscapes→Mapillary, and intra-dataset UVO demonstrate improvements in AP100 and AR100 over several baselines, including Mask2Former and two-stage methods like LDET and GGN.

Strengths  
* The tackled problem is challenging and of strong interest to the community. OWIS goes beyond closed-world segmentation by requiring discovery of novel object classes at test time, which is a far-from-solved challenge.  
* The idea of using a cross-task consistency loss between a foreground prediction branch and instance masks is intuitively appealing. By encouraging the union of predicted masks to match a class-agnostic foreground map, the method can down-weight erroneous negatives arising from missing annotations.  
* The semi-supervised extension is practical: by treating entirely unlabeled images as the extreme case of incomplete annotation, the same consistency constraint can be applied without additional loss terms. The experiments on UVO with 30% labeled images show that semi-TOIS outperforms its fully-supervised counterpart trained on 50% labels, underscoring the potential annotation savings.  
* Empirical results are solid and reproducible. The authors report significant AP100 gains—up to +4.75% in UVO→UVO and +4.05% in COCO→UVO settings—and release code in their supplementary material.  

Weaknesses  
* Problem statement description could be improved. The introduction (Section 1) dives into OWIS definitions and Mask2Former adaptation without clearly articulating the gap in current two-stage versus single-stage approaches. For instance, a clearer comparison between how two-stage bounding-box proposals fail under unseen categories versus the end-to-end prediction in Mask2Former would ground the reader before presenting TOIS.  
* It is hard to grasp from the paper what are the authors’ concrete contributions beyond re-labeling Mask2Former as TOIS. The manuscript states “building upon a recently-proposed close-world segmentation method of Mask2Former” but does not sufficiently distinguish which architectural elements are truly novel (the foreground branch?) versus which are inherited.  
* The authors rename the Mask2Former architecture as TOIS and appear to claim it as a contribution, even though the core transformer encoder–decoder and mask-classification formulation remain unchanged. The only substantive modification is the addition of a small FCN for foreground prediction and a consistency regularizer. As the authors themselves note, “once training is done, we discard this branch,” raising the question of whether the foreground head is a mere auxiliary or a genuine architectural advance.  
* While the cross-task consistency loss is an interesting idea, its novelty is incremental—it can be viewed as a form of self-supervision or pseudo-labeling (reducing false negatives) rather than a fundamentally new segmentation paradigm.  

Detailed Comments  
* Benchmarks and datasets should be clearly introduced when first mentioned. In the Abstract, COCO and UVO appear without context. A brief parenthetical (e.g., “COCO [Lin et al. 2014] and UVO [Wang et al. 2021] datasets”) would aid readers unfamiliar with OWIS benchmarks.  
* Figure 1 is overly congested. It combines annotation examples, motivation diagrams, visual results, and AP curves (parts a–g) all in one. Splitting it into two figures—one for motivation and architecture visualization (a–c) and another for quantitative results (d–g)—would improve readability. In part (b), “consistency” is misspelled, and “Cityscapes” is misspelled in the caption. Moreover, subfigures 1c–1f are never explicitly referenced in the text.  
* Figure 2 lacks clarity. The backbone and flow of feature maps through the three branches are not clearly annotated. The figure caption states, “the foreground prediction branch segments a foreground region,” which is tautological. Instead, it should specify that the input to the FCN is the multi-scale feature maps from the transformer decoder and that its output is a binary map aligned to the image grid. Notations such as Ki (the number of annotated instances in image i) and the meaning of N object queries in Section 3.2 should be explicitly defined in the text to avoid confusion.  
* In Section 3.2, Ki is introduced as “each mask is multiplied by a weighting score … indicating if a mask should be selected as an instance mask … Ki is the number of annotated object instances.” This sentence is cumbersome. A separate line defining Ki, perhaps in a table of symbols, would help.  
* The language and structure of the manuscript could benefit from a thorough copyedit. There are multiple typos, missing references (e.g., when mentioning “benchmarks” or earlier OWIS works, add citations), and inconsistent notation (sometimes using v for objectness targets, other times s).  

Clarity, Quality, and Reproducibility  
Clarity: The overall presentation is uneven. Key terms such as “foreground map” and “cross-task consistency” appear early but are only fully explained later in Section 3.3. The paper would benefit from an early roadmap that outlines the three branches and the loss terms in a unified notation.  
Quality: While the experimental methodology is sound—reporting intra- and cross-dataset performance, ablations on pseudo-labeling, foreground branch, and consistency loss—the writing occasionally obscures insights. For example, in Section 4.4, the statement “pseudo-labeling causes performance degradation on UVO” is interesting but not discussed in depth; an analysis of how noisy annotations in UVO impact the pseudo-label generator would strengthen the argument.  
Reproducibility: The authors build on Detectron2 and provide implementation details (learning rates, backbones, Cutout augmentation). They also describe pseudo-label generation thresholds (confidence > 0.8, IOU > 0.2) and maintain code availability. These specifics should allow others to replicate results.  

Novelty  
The paper’s main novelty lies in adapting Mask2Former to the open-world setting via an auxiliary foreground head and a consistency loss. However, this is a relatively modest extension rather than a radically new architecture. The cross-task loss resembles existing ideas in self-supervised segmentation where consistency across views or tasks is enforced. The semi-supervised scenario is valuable, but the approach—training with supervision on labeled images and only the consistency loss on unlabeled ones—follows established semi-supervised paradigms (e.g., mean-teacher, fixed auxiliary losses).  

Overall Assessment  
While the proposed TOIS framework demonstrates measurable improvements on OWIS benchmarks and introduces a practical consistency regularizer, the paper’s contributions are largely incremental on top of Mask2Former. The novelty is limited to an auxiliary branch and loss term, and the presentation would benefit from clearer problem framing, more precise definition of terms, and improved figure design. Given the challenges of the open-world segmentation task, the work is of interest, but it falls short of a sufficiently substantial methodological advance. Therefore, I recommend a marginal decision: this paper is positioned “just above the acceptance threshold.”  

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an open world instance segmentation framework, which contains three stages: 1) it proposes an auxiliary task of predicting total foreground on the basis of existing query-based mask2former model. 2) it includes a cross-task consistency loss and improve results by correcting outputs between two tasks. 3) the paper also designs a way of adding pseudo labels on COCO, which gain some improvement on open-world settings, including semi-supervised setting.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Pros:

1. The paper proposes a single-stage open-world Instance segmentation framework based on Mask2Former.
2. Adding an auxiliary task and a consistency loss for self-supervised learning, which improves a lot on both UVO-to-UVO and COCO-to-UVO settings.
3. Experiments demonstrate the semi-supervised framework can achieve remarkable performance with much smaller amount of labeled data.

Cons:
1. Although the paper proposed a new constraint called “cross-task consistency loss” for OWIS problem, it lacks of sufficient validation by either theoretical interpretation or experimental evidence how the cross-task consistency loss works for providing an error-correcting mechanism for handling noisy annotations. How does the “cross-task consistency loss” help to handle and separate neighboring occlusion objects?

2. The tech novelty of the paper is weak and incremental. Being first to adapt Mask2Former to OWIS is an incremental idea. Also, it should be called query-based or transformer-based, instead of one-stage. Can the author give proof on why the auxiliary task of predicting total foreground mask will help novel object detection?

3. Missing comparison/discussion with existing semi-supervised instance segmentation methods, such as [a, b].

[a] Learning to segment every thing. CVPR, 2018.

[b] Shapemask: Learning to segment novel objects by refining shape priors. ICCV, 2019.

4. Missing two-stage works on Closed-world instance segmentation, such as [c, d, e].

[c] PointRend: Image Segmentation as Rendering. CVPR, 2019.

[d] Deep occlusion-aware instance segmentation with overlapping bilayers. CVPR, 2021.

[e] Boundary-preserving Mask R-CNN. ECCV, 2020.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Good paper structure. Figure 5 is blurry.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Limited technical novelty. Deeper theoretical analysis is desired. My rating will be adjusted according to the author response.
